proposals.title: Temporal Embedding Dynamics: Interpreting Foundation Model Representations for Robustness to Clinical Concept Drift
proposals.motivation: Electronic Health Record (EHR) data is subject to clinical concept drift, where the statistical properties of medical codes, lab tests, and treatment patterns evolve over time due to changes in practice, coding guidelines, and disease prevalence. This drift degrades the performance of static machine learning models, threatening their long-term clinical utility. Foundation models trained on longitudinal EHR data have shown empirical robustness to such drift, but the mechanisms behind this robustness are not understood.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that foundation models encode clinical concepts not as static embeddings, but as trajectories within a latent space, where the direction and magnitude of a concept's drift over time are explicitly captured. This learned representation of temporal dynamics is the key mechanism conferring robustness to distribution shift.
proposals.method: We will analyze the learned representations of a pre-trained clinical foundation model (e.g., based on a Transformer architecture like BEHRT or a T5-style model) using Steinberg's 'meds_reader' library for efficient data extraction. For a set of key clinical concepts (e.g., diagnosis codes, medications), we will track their contextual embeddings across patient records from different temporal windows (e.g., 2010-2014 vs. 2015-2019). We will apply dimensionality reduction (t-SNE, UMAP) and trajectory analysis to visualize and quantify concept drift in the latent space. We will then use probing classifiers and saliency methods (e.g., integrated gradients) to interpret which temporal patterns in the input sequence (context) most influence the embedding's position.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first interpretable framework for how foundation models internally represent temporal change, moving beyond black-box robustness claims. It will yield actionable insights for model auditing and for designing training strategies that explicitly optimize for temporal invariance. The findings will advance methods for building reliable, long-lived clinical prediction systems.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has evaluated model performance on temporally split data, no study has directly analyzed the *evolving internal representations* of clinical concepts to explain robustness. This proposal uniquely bridges the interpretability of learned embeddings with the practical problem of concept drift in medicine.

proposals.title: CLINICAL-REASON: Designing Clinically-Grounded Pretraining Objectives for Electronic Health Record Foundation Models
proposals.motivation: Current foundation models for Electronic Health Records (EHRs) are predominantly pretrained using next-code or masked-code prediction, objectives derived from language modeling that lack explicit clinical grounding. This misalignment may limit a model's ability to learn the complex temporal, causal, and decision-making patterns inherent in real-world clinical medicine, ultimately constraining performance on downstream predictive and reasoning tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pretraining EHR foundation models on novel objectives designed to mimic core clinical reasoning processes—such as predicting clinical outcomes from partial sequences or inferring latent patient states—will yield representations that significantly outperform those from standard next-code prediction on a range of complex clinical tasks.
proposals.method: We will design and implement three novel pretraining tasks within a transformer-based architecture: 1) Time-to-Event Forecasting, predicting the time until a future clinical event (e.g., readmission) from a visit sequence; 2) Clinical Abductive Reasoning, masking a critical intermediate code (e.g., a diagnosis) and training the model to infer it from preceding and subsequent context; and 3) Intervention-Outcome Association, learning to associate sets of medications or procedures with their expected lab trend outcomes. These tasks will be integrated into a multi-task pretraining framework using the meds_reader library for efficient data processing. We will evaluate the learned representations on benchmarks from Steinberg's prior work, including long-context clinical prediction tasks and time-to-event analysis for 3D imaging.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic framework for developing clinically-meaningful pretraining objectives for EHRs. It will deliver a new, publicly available pretrained model and establish which objective functions best transfer to critical tasks like prognosis, phenotyping, and counterfactual reasoning, moving the field beyond linguistic analogies.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has adapted language model objectives to EHRs, we propose the first dedicated suite of pretraining tasks explicitly derived from clinical reasoning paradigms (e.g., prognosis, abductive diagnosis, treatment effect estimation), moving beyond syntactic code prediction to model semantic clinical pathways.

proposals.title: Cross-Site Generalization of EHR Foundation Models: A Systematic Evaluation of Representation Transfer for Clinical Prediction Tasks
proposals.motivation: Foundation models trained on Electronic Health Records (EHRs) promise to improve clinical predictions, but their real-world utility depends on generalizing across healthcare systems with differing patient populations, coding practices, and data structures. Current evaluations, like those in Steinberg's 'Context Clues' paper, often assess performance within a single institution, leaving a critical gap in understanding cross-institutional robustness. This limits the scalability and equitable deployment of these models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that representations from a foundation model pre-trained on a large, source-hospital EHR will transfer effectively to target hospitals for downstream tasks, but that performance degradation will be systematically linked to specific data shifts (e.g., label prevalence, clinical vocabulary, and temporal context patterns).
proposals.method: We will adapt the 'meds_reader' library for efficient, standardized processing of raw EHR data from MIMIC-IV and a second, distinct health system (e.g., eICU or a private partner dataset). Using a transformer-based architecture pre-trained via masked language modeling on the source data, we will freeze the encoder and train lightweight task-specific heads on the source. We will then evaluate these fixed representations on held-out target data for tasks like mortality prediction and readmission, quantifying performance drop. We will further analyze the relationship between transfer performance and measurable dataset discrepancies using the 'context' and 'records' structure from prior work.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic framework for benchmarking the cross-site transferability of EHR foundation models, moving beyond single-institution validation. It will produce actionable insights into which data characteristics most hinder generalization, guiding future pre-training strategies and evaluation standards for clinically deployable models.
proposals.novelty: While transfer learning is well-studied in computer vision, its systematic evaluation for EHR foundation models across real, heterogeneous health systems is novel. This work uniquely combines Steinberg's expertise in long-context model evaluation and efficient EHR processing to tackle the pragmatic problem of model portability, a prerequisite for widespread clinical adoption.

proposals.title: Transformer EHR: Replacing Sequential Encoders with Long-Context Models for Clinical Prediction
proposals.motivation: Electronic Health Records (EHRs) represent patient histories as long, irregular sequences of clinical events. Traditional RNN/LSTM encoders struggle with these long-range dependencies due to vanishing gradients and computational inefficiency, potentially limiting predictive performance for complex outcomes. The recent emergence of efficient Transformer architectures (e.g., Longformer, Performer) in NLP, designed to handle long sequences, offers a promising but underexplored alternative for structured EHR modeling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that replacing RNN/LSTM encoders with modern, long-context Transformer architectures will more effectively capture long-range dependencies in patient timelines, leading to superior performance on challenging clinical prediction tasks that require integrating information across years of patient history.
proposals.method: We will adapt encoder-only Transformer models with efficient attention mechanisms (e.g., sliding window, linear attention) to process structured, time-stamped EHR sequences. Using the `meds_reader` library for fast data processing, we will format patient histories into sequences of medical codes with temporal offsets. We will pre-train the Transformer using a time-to-event objective on a large-scale EHR corpus, then fine-tune and benchmark against state-of-the-art RNN baselines on key tasks from MIMIC-IV, such as in-hospital mortality and prolonged length-of-stay prediction. Model analysis will focus on attention patterns to interpret captured dependencies.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, head-to-head comparison of Transformer vs. RNN architectures for longitudinal EHR modeling, establishing a new performance benchmark. It will deliver an open-source, pre-trained Transformer encoder tailored for clinical timelines, enabling more accurate predictions for outcomes dependent on distant patient history.
proposals.novelty: While Transformers are used in clinical NLP for notes, their systematic application as a direct replacement for RNNs in modeling structured, time-series EHR data is novel. This proposal uniquely integrates efficient long-context attention, time-aware pre-training, and the `meds_reader` ecosystem for a foundation model approach to patient sequence encoding.

proposals.title: MetaPromptEHR: Prototypical Networks and Prompt Tuning for Few-Shot Clinical Prediction from Electronic Health Records
proposals.motivation: Developing robust clinical prediction models typically requires large, labeled datasets, which are scarce for rare conditions or novel healthcare tasks. This data bottleneck limits the rapid deployment of AI in clinical settings and hinders research on specialized patient cohorts. Few-shot learning offers a principled framework to build predictive models from only a handful of examples, directly addressing this critical limitation in medical AI.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a meta-learning framework, specifically prototypical networks, combined with prompt-based fine-tuning of a pre-trained clinical language model, will significantly outperform standard fine-tuning and data augmentation baselines on few-shot clinical prediction tasks using longitudinal EHR data.
proposals.method: We will leverage the meds_reader library to process longitudinal EHR sequences into a structured context. A foundation model pre-trained on clinical text (e.g., ClinicalBERT or BioBERT) will serve as our encoder. We will implement a prototypical network meta-learning algorithm, where the model learns to compute embeddings such that examples from the same clinical class cluster around a prototype. For adaptation to a new task, we will employ prompt-tuning methods, formulating the prediction as a cloze-style task to guide the pre-trained model with minimal parameter updates, rather than full fine-tuning.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, open-source framework for few-shot learning on structured EHR timelines, extending the utility of pre-trained clinical models. It will establish benchmarks for few-shot clinical prediction, demonstrating practical viability for low-resource scenarios. The integration of meta-learning with prompt engineering offers a novel methodological pathway for efficient model adaptation in healthcare.
proposals.novelty: While few-shot learning and prompt-tuning have been explored separately in NLP, their synergistic application to structured, longitudinal EHR data for clinical prediction is novel. This approach uniquely combines the representation power of meta-learning with the parameter efficiency of prompt-based methods, tailored for the complex context of medical records.

proposals.title: PERSIST: Personalized, Event-Responsive State Inference for Streaming EHRs
proposals.motivation: Current foundation models for Electronic Health Records (EHRs) are typically trained on population-level data and applied in a static, batch-oriented manner. This limits their utility for real-time, personalized patient monitoring, where a patient's clinical state must be continuously updated with each new lab result, note, or medication. A method for learning dynamic, individual-specific representations that evolve with the clinical timeline is crucial for timely intervention and precision medicine.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a patient-specific, continuously updating representation model, trained via a self-supervised objective on streaming EHR events, will yield more accurate and timely predictions for clinical deterioration than static, population-based embeddings.
proposals.method: We will adapt and extend a time-to-event pretraining framework (inspired by Steinberg's prior work) to the longitudinal EHR context. The core architecture will be a transformer encoder that processes a patient's event sequence (via meds_reader) using a sliding context window. A recurrent or momentum-updated memory module will maintain a persistent patient state vector. The model will be trained using a masked event prediction task and a contrastive loss that distinguishes a patient's future state from others, enabling it to learn personalized dynamics. Inference will involve a lightweight forward pass to update the state with each new event.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel modeling paradigm for 'scale-down' personalized representation learning from streaming EHRs, 2) a benchmark for evaluating real-time predictive performance on tasks like sepsis or readmission onset, and 3) an open-source framework for continuous patient state inference that integrates with existing EHR processing pipelines.
proposals.novelty: While existing work focuses on long-context modeling for retrospective prediction, our approach uniquely shifts the focus to efficient, continuous state updates for a single patient, moving from a population foundation to a personalized, real-time inference engine. This combines ideas from time-to-event learning, online learning, and patient-specific modeling in a novel way.

proposals.title: Generative EHR: Synthesizing Realistic Patient Timelines via Inversion of a Pretrained Clinical Representation Model
proposals.motivation: The development and validation of clinical AI models are bottlenecked by the scarcity of large, diverse, and privacy-compliant Electronic Health Record (EHR) datasets. Current synthetic data generation methods, like GANs or rule-based models, often fail to capture the complex temporal dependencies and multi-modal nature of real patient records. A method to generate high-fidelity, privacy-preserving synthetic EHRs would accelerate research while adhering to data governance standards.
proposals.hypothesis: A deep representation model, pretrained on real EHRs for a clinical prediction task, encodes a sufficiently rich and structured latent space of patient trajectories that can be sampled and decoded to generate novel, realistic, and temporally coherent synthetic patient records.
proposals.method: We will leverage the encoder of a pre-trained time-to-event or long-context transformer model (e.g., from Steinberg's prior work) as a fixed feature extractor. We will train a conditional decoder, likely a transformer or diffusion model, to map from sampled points in this learned latent space and a conditioning vector (e.g., initial demographics) back to sequences of structured medical codes, lab values, and notes. The process will be guided by adversarial and reconstruction losses against real data, with explicit privacy constraints via differential privacy or latent space filtering to prevent memorization.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, model-based pipeline for generating longitudinal synthetic EHRs that preserve clinical utility for downstream task training. It will establish evaluation benchmarks for synthetic EHR fidelity, focusing on temporal dynamics and statistical properties. The resulting method will be released as an open-source extension to the `meds_reader` library.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior generative models trained directly on raw data, this approach inverts a clinically meaningful representation space, explicitly leveraging the structured knowledge embedded in a model pretrained for a medical outcome. This focuses the generation on clinically plausible patterns rather than just statistical co-occurrences.

proposals.title: Task-Specific Clinical Intelligence: A Framework for Developing and Validating Compact, High-Performance Models for Narrow EHR Prediction Tasks
proposals.motivation: The clinical AI field is increasingly dominated by massive, general-purpose foundation models for EHRs, which demand immense computational resources, extensive data curation, and raise deployment challenges in resource-constrained healthcare settings. This paradigm may be inefficient for many well-defined, high-stakes clinical tasks where interpretability, low latency, and reliability are paramount. A systematic investigation into the performance ceiling of highly specialized, lightweight models is needed to challenge the assumed necessity of scale for all clinical prediction problems.
proposals.hypothesis: For narrowly defined, high-impact clinical prediction tasks (e.g., in-hospital mortality, specific decompensation, readmission for a particular condition), small, task-specific models trained on curated, domain-informed features can match or exceed the performance of general clinical foundation models, while offering superior computational efficiency and practical deployability.
proposals.method: We will select 3-5 specific clinical tasks from MIMIC-IV and leverage the meds_reader library for efficient, standardized EHR feature extraction. For each task, we will design a compact model architecture (e.g., a small transformer or a carefully engineered gradient-boosted tree ensemble) trained exclusively on relevant, temporally contextualized data windows. Performance will be benchmarked against a state-of-the-art general clinical foundation model (e.g., a pretrained model akin to those evaluated in 'Context Clues') on held-out test sets, with rigorous evaluation of AUC, calibration, and inference speed. We will analyze the trade-offs between performance, model size, and data requirements.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete framework and evidence base for developing efficient, specialized clinical AI, challenging the one-size-fits-all foundation model approach. It will deliver publicly available, validated compact models for specific tasks and establish clear benchmarks for the trade-offs between model generality and specialized performance, guiding resource allocation in clinical AI research and deployment.
proposals.novelty: While model specialization is known, this proposal directly and systematically contests the prevailing foundation model trajectory in clinical AI by demonstrating that for critical narrow tasks, extreme specialization can be superior, an argument supported by preliminary analyses but not yet rigorously proven against modern long-context clinical LLMs.

proposals.title: ARCH-EMR: A Controlled Benchmark for Architectural Choices in Electronic Medical Record Modeling
proposals.motivation: The rapid adoption of foundation models for clinical prediction tasks has outpaced systematic understanding of which architectural components are most effective for Electronic Medical Record (EMR) data. While recent work has focused on scaling models and context length, there is a lack of controlled, head-to-head comparisons of core architectural paradigms (e.g., Transformers vs. structured state-space models, different temporal encoding schemes) on standardized clinical tasks. This gap hinders efficient model development and deployment in resource-constrained medical settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that architectural choices related to temporal modeling and long-range dependency capture will have a more significant impact on clinical prediction performance than model scale alone, and that an optimal architecture exists for specific clinical task families (e.g., time-to-event vs. multi-label classification).
proposals.method: We will conduct a rigorous ablation study using the MIMIC-IV and a proprietary EHR dataset, processed via the meds_reader library for consistency. We will train and evaluate a suite of models on identical data, holding compute and pretraining constant. Architectures will include: 1) Standard Transformer variants (BERT, GPT-style), 2) Long-context Transformers (e.g., Longformer, Perceiver), 3) Structured State Space Sequence models (e.g., S4, Mamba), and 4) Hybrid models with explicit temporal modules. Evaluation will span tasks from Steinberg's prior work: in-hospital mortality, readmission, and time-to-event prediction for disease progression.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, controlled benchmark for EMR model architectures, yielding clear design principles for the community. It will produce a public leaderboard and codebase to standardize future comparisons, moving the field beyond ad-hoc architectural selection.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior studies focused on scaling or specific model families, this proposal systematically isolates architectural variables across diverse modern sequence models in a controlled clinical setting, directly building on but extending the context-length analysis of Steinberg's 'Context Clues' work.

proposals.title: ContiNet: A Continent-Scale Foundation Model for Electronic Medical Records
proposals.motivation: Current clinical foundation models are typically trained on data from single institutions or small consortia, limiting their generalizability and robustness to diverse patient populations, coding practices, and healthcare workflows. Scaling model training to a continent-spanning dataset is critical to create a truly universal medical AI that can perform robustly across varied healthcare systems and demographics, a recognized frontier in clinical AI as of 2025. This scale directly addresses the data fragmentation problem in healthcare AI, potentially unlocking more reliable and equitable predictive tools.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training a single transformer-based foundation model on a standardized, continent-scale Electronic Medical Record (EMR) dataset will yield a model with superior generalizability and performance on diverse clinical prediction tasks, compared to models trained on single-institution or smaller multi-institutional datasets, by learning a more comprehensive representation of medical concepts and temporal patient trajectories.
proposals.method: We will leverage the `meds_reader` library to efficiently process and standardize longitudinal EMR data from hundreds of hospitals across a defined region (e.g., a national health network). We will employ a causal transformer architecture, similar to GPT-style models but adapted for structured, time-stamped event sequences. The model will be pretrained using a masked event prediction objective on this vast corpus. Following the precedent of 'Time-to-Event Pretraining', we will incorporate a secondary pretraining task for time-to-next-event prediction to enhance temporal reasoning. The resulting 'ContiNet' model will then be evaluated on held-out hospitals and benchmark clinical tasks using the rigorous, long-context evaluation framework established in 'Context Clues'.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first publicly documented foundation model trained on a continent-scale EMR dataset, establishing a new benchmark for scale in clinical AI. It will provide empirical evidence on the relationship between training data diversity/volume and model generalizability in the medical domain. The project will also produce a scalable technical blueprint and open-source tools for federated data standardization and large-scale clinical model training.
proposals.novelty: While multi-hospital studies exist, the proposed scale of hundreds of institutions for training a single, dense foundation model is unprecedented in clinical AI research as of 2025. The integration of continent-scale data engineering with a tailored, time-aware transformer pretraining strategy moves beyond proof-of-concept single-site models towards a practical, general-purpose clinical foundation model.

proposals.title: Adapt or Prompt? A Systematic Benchmark of Personalization Techniques for Clinical Foundation Models on EHR Tasks
proposals.motivation: Clinical foundation models (CFMs) pretrained on large-scale electronic health records (EHRs) show strong general performance, but adapting them to specific clinical tasks or patient subpopulations remains a critical challenge. Efficient adaptation is essential for real-world deployment, where data for a target task (e.g., a rare disease) is often limited. A systematic comparison of modern parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods—like adapters, prompt-tuning, and few-shot fine-tuning—for clinical tasks is lacking, leaving practitioners without clear guidance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for clinical prediction tasks on structured EHRs, lightweight adapter-based methods will outperform prompt-tuning in low-data regimes due to their ability to preserve and specialize pretrained temporal representations, and that the optimal technique will vary based on task complexity and available context length.
proposals.method: We will benchmark three PEFT families: (1) Few-shot full fine-tuning, (2) Prompt-tuning (soft prompts prepended to input), and (3) Adapter methods (e.g., LoRA, Houlsby adapters). Using the `meds_reader` library, we will process longitudinal EHR data from MIMIC-IV. We will adapt a pre-existing long-context clinical transformer (e.g., based on Steinberg's prior work) on diverse tasks: mortality prediction, readmission, and specific phenotype classification. Experiments will systematically vary the amount of target-task training data (from 10-shot to 1000 examples) and evaluate using time-to-event and standard classification metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive benchmark of PEFT methods for clinical NLP, yielding practical guidelines for model personalization in data-scarce medical settings. It will also release an open-source framework for adapting CFMs, accelerating applied clinical AI research.
proposals.novelty: While PEFT methods are studied in general NLP, their systematic evaluation on longitudinal, structured EHR data with clinical foundation models is novel. This work uniquely integrates the challenges of medical context length, temporal reasoning, and data scarcity into the adaptation benchmark.

proposals.title: MAML for EHRs: Meta-Learning for Few-Shot Adaptation of Clinical Foundation Models
proposals.motivation: Clinical foundation models (CFMs) trained on large-scale electronic health records (EHRs) show promise but struggle with data-scarce tasks like rare disease prediction or specialized outcome forecasting, where labeled examples are few. Current fine-tuning approaches require substantial task-specific data, limiting real-world deployment. A method for rapid adaptation with minimal labeled episodes is critically needed to make CFMs practically useful across diverse clinical scenarios.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) to a pre-trained clinical foundation model will enable rapid, data-efficient adaptation to novel clinical prediction tasks, achieving superior few-shot performance compared to standard fine-tuning or linear probing baselines.
proposals.method: We will leverage a pre-trained CFM (e.g., from Steinberg's prior work on long-context EHR models) as the base model. Using MAML, we will meta-train it on a distribution of diverse, existing clinical tasks (e.g., readmission, mortality, phenotype classification) constructed from the MIMIC-IV dataset. The inner-loop adaptation will simulate few-shot learning scenarios (e.g., 5-10 support samples), and the outer loop will update the model's initial parameters for rapid generalization. We will evaluate on held-out, novel clinical tasks using few-shot episodes, benchmarking against fine-tuning and a non-meta-learned initialization.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a meta-learned CFM capable of few-shot adaptation, a benchmark suite for few-shot clinical task evaluation, and empirical evidence on the data efficiency gains of meta-learning in the clinical domain. It will advance the practical utility of foundation models for low-resource clinical settings.
proposals.novelty: While MAML is established in computer vision, its application to complex, longitudinal EHR data and a clinical foundation model is novel. This bridges the gap between generic meta-learning and the specific challenges of structured, temporal medical records, moving beyond single-task fine-tuning paradigms dominant in clinical AI pre-2026.

proposals.title: Temporal Knowledge Graph Foundation Models for EHRs: A Graph Neural Network Approach
proposals.motivation: Electronic Health Records (EHRs) are inherently multimodal, relational, and temporal, but current foundation modeling approaches often treat them as sequential token streams, losing rich structural semantics. Modeling EHRs as temporal knowledge graphs (TKGs) can explicitly capture relationships (e.g., drug-prescribed_for-disease) and their evolution, a critical need for clinical prediction tasks. This structured representation aligns with the relational nature of medicine and could improve model interpretability and performance on downstream tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a foundation model pre-trained on patient-specific temporal knowledge graphs, using graph neural network architectures, will outperform sequential, language-model-based approaches on key clinical prediction tasks by better capturing the relational and temporal structure of medical data.
proposals.method: We will use the meds_reader library to process raw EHR data into a TKG schema with time-stamped nodes (e.g., medications, diagnoses, labs) and edges (e.g., 'treats', 'follows', 'indicates'). We will apply a temporal graph neural network (TGNN) architecture, such as a Recurrent Event Graph Network (REGN) or a Temporal Graph Attention Network, to learn dynamic node embeddings. This model will be pre-trained using a combination of masked node/edge prediction and a novel time-to-event forecasting objective, inspired by Steinberg's prior work, to learn robust patient representations.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a scalable framework for constructing patient TKGs from raw EHRs, 2) a novel TGNN-based foundation model for clinical data, and 3) a rigorous evaluation benchmark comparing graph-based and sequence-based models on diverse clinical tasks (e.g., readmission, mortality) using real-world EHR datasets, extending the 'Context Clues' analysis to structural models.
proposals.novelty: While GNNs have been applied to static medical graphs, their integration as a pre-trained foundation model on dynamic, patient-specific TKGs is novel. This moves beyond treating EHRs as documents or images, explicitly leveraging graph structure and temporal dynamics for general-purpose clinical representation learning.

proposals.title: Causal Treatment-Outcome Discovery from Electronic Health Records via Foundation Model Representations
proposals.motivation: Estimating the causal effect of treatments from observational EHR data is critical for informing clinical practice but is confounded by complex, high-dimensional patient histories. Traditional methods rely on strong, often untestable, assumptions about the causal graph or require manual variable selection. Foundation models trained on EHRs offer rich, contextualized patient representations that may encode latent confounders, providing a new substrate for data-driven causal discovery.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying causal discovery algorithms to the latent representations from a clinical foundation model will reveal more plausible and stable causal relationships between treatments and outcomes than discovery applied to raw EHR features or manual variable sets.
proposals.method: We will leverage a pre-trained clinical foundation model (e.g., based on Steinberg's work with long-context models) to generate dynamic, patient-level representations from structured EHR sequences. Using these representations as input variables, we will apply constraint-based causal discovery algorithms, specifically the PC and FCI algorithms, to learn a partial ancestral graph of treatment-outcome relationships. We will validate the inferred graphs using established clinical knowledge and test the stability of discovered edges across patient subgroups and temporal partitions of the data.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel methodology for data-driven causal hypothesis generation from complex EHRs. It will provide empirical evidence on the utility of foundation model embeddings for improving causal discovery. The resulting framework could help clinicians and researchers identify potential treatment mechanisms and confounding pathways for further rigorous study.
proposals.novelty: While causal discovery has been applied to EHRs, the integration of foundation model representations as a principled, high-level feature space for discovery is novel. This approach moves beyond using raw codes or manual summaries, leveraging the model's inherent contextual understanding to mitigate hidden confounding in the discovery process.

proposals.title: Dual-Bench: A Zero-Shot Clinical Reasoning Benchmark for Foundation Models in Electronic Health Records
proposals.motivation: Foundation models for Electronic Health Records (EHRs) are typically evaluated via fine-tuning on labeled datasets, which conflates task-specific learning with inherent clinical reasoning ability. This masks a model's true capacity for generalization and its potential for deployment in low-resource clinical settings where labeled data is scarce. A rigorous benchmark for zero-shot reasoning is needed to assess whether these models can perform clinically relevant tasks using only their pretrained knowledge and task instructions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that current long-context foundation models, despite strong performance on fine-tuned tasks, will demonstrate significant and systematic gaps in zero-shot clinical reasoning on EHRs, particularly on tasks requiring temporal inference and complex concept mapping.
proposals.method: We will develop Dual-Bench, a benchmark comprising diverse clinical prediction tasks (e.g., readmission, mortality, phenotype classification) derived from public EHR datasets like MIMIC-IV. For each task, we will formulate a natural language instruction and present the model with structured patient timelines processed via the meds_reader library. We will evaluate state-of-the-art long-context models (e.g., GPT-4, Claude 3, and specialized models from Steinberg's prior work) strictly in a zero-shot setting, providing no examples. Performance will be compared against a fine-tuned baseline to quantify the 'reasoning gap'.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first standardized benchmark for evaluating zero-shot clinical reasoning in EHR foundation models, establishing a clearer measure of their inherent medical knowledge and instruction-following capability. It will identify specific failure modes in clinical inference, guiding future pretraining and architectural improvements. The benchmark and analysis will be released as an open-source package to facilitate community-wide evaluation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (e.g., 'Context Clues') evaluates models on long clinical contexts, it does so primarily in a fine-tuned or few-shot setting. Our proposal uniquely isolates and stresses the zero-shot reasoning capacity, which is a more direct test of a model's foundational medical understanding and its utility for rapid, label-efficient deployment.

proposals.title: The Clinical Prediction Atlas: A Scalable Benchmark of 500+ Tasks for Holistic Foundation Model Evaluation
proposals.motivation: Current clinical AI benchmarks are narrow, focusing on a handful of tasks like mortality or readmission, which fails to capture the true breadth of clinical reasoning needed for real-world deployment. This limits our ability to comprehensively evaluate the generalization and utility of emerging foundation models trained on electronic health records (EHRs). A massive, diverse benchmark is needed to rigorously assess model capabilities across the full spectrum of clinical prediction, from diagnosis and prognosis to treatment and resource use.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a foundation model's performance on a curated set of hundreds of diverse clinical tasks will reveal its true generalization capabilities and latent clinical knowledge, and that performance will correlate strongly with model scale and pre-training data diversity.
proposals.method: We will leverage the MIMIC-IV and All of Us datasets to define over 500 prediction tasks spanning multiple time horizons (in-hospital, 30-day, 1-year), clinical domains (cardiology, oncology, etc.), and prediction types (diagnosis, intervention, outcome, utilization). Tasks will be formulated using the OHDSI/OMOP common data model for consistency. We will use the meds_reader library for efficient, standardized data extraction and preprocessing. Models, including both specialized architectures (e.g., RNNs, transformers) and general-purpose LLMs adapted for long clinical context, will be evaluated in a standardized zero-shot and fine-tuned framework to assess task breadth scalability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first large-scale, multi-domain benchmark for clinical prediction, enabling systematic evaluation of foundation models. It will establish performance baselines across hundreds of tasks and identify which model architectures and pre-training strategies generalize best. The benchmark will serve as a foundational tool for the community to guide future model development.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (e.g., Steinberg's 'Context Clues') evaluates models on a few long-context tasks, this proposal scales task breadth by two orders of magnitude, creating a holistic evaluation suite. It systematically connects diverse prediction types into a single benchmark, moving beyond siloed evaluations.

proposals.title: Clinician-in-the-Loop Active Learning for Prioritizing Novel Gene-Disease Associations from Integrated Genomic and Phenotypic EHR Data
proposals.motivation: Despite advances in genomic sequencing, a vast number of gene-disease associations remain undiscovered, hindering diagnosis and treatment. Current computational methods generate candidate links but are bottlenecked by the need for expert validation, which is a scarce resource. An efficient, strategic system to prioritize candidates for expert review is urgently needed to accelerate translational discovery in clinical genomics.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating an active learning framework with a predictive model for gene-disease associations will significantly improve the efficiency of expert validation by strategically selecting the most informative and uncertain candidates, thereby accelerating the discovery of novel, clinically relevant links.
proposals.method: We will first train a predictive model (e.g., a graph neural network or transformer) on established gene-disease knowledge bases (e.g., OMIM, ClinVar) and patient-level data from EHRs, leveraging Steinberg's 'meds_reader' for efficient processing of phenotypic 'context'. This model will score novel candidate associations. An active learning loop, using an acquisition function like Bayesian Active Learning by Disagreement (BALD), will then select high-uncertainty/high-impact predictions. These will be presented via a structured interface to clinicians for annotation ('labels'), and the feedback 'records' will be used to iteratively retrain the model and refine discovery criteria.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, scalable framework for expert-driven genomic discovery, directly applicable to hospital biobanks. It will yield a validated set of high-priority novel gene-disease hypotheses and a methodological advance in integrating active learning with clinical prediction tasks on long-context EHR data.
proposals.novelty: This approach uniquely fuses active learning—common in imaging—with the specific challenges of gene-disease discovery from longitudinal EHRs, creating a closed-loop system where clinician feedback directly shapes the genomic discovery model, moving beyond static prediction.

proposals.title: ActiveRankEHR: An Adaptive Query-by-Committee Framework for Efficient Refinement of Clinical Ranking Models
proposals.motivation: Clinical ranking models, such as those prioritizing patients for specialist review or predicting disease progression, require large, expertly labeled datasets for training. Acquiring such labels is a major bottleneck, as clinician time is scarce and expensive. Existing methods for integrating feedback are often inefficient, failing to strategically target the most informative cases for expert review, which limits the practical scalability and accuracy of AI-assisted clinical decision tools.
proposals.hypothesis: An active learning framework based on Query-by-Committee (QBC), which prioritizes clinician feedback on patient cases where an ensemble of ranking models shows high predictive disagreement, will refine model accuracy more efficiently than standard passive learning or uncertainty sampling alone.
proposals.method: We will develop an ensemble of 3-5 clinical ranking models, initialized from a foundation model like ClinicalBERT or fine-tuned on EHR data using the `meds_reader` library. For each candidate case in an unlabeled pool, we will compute a disagreement measure (e.g., variance in predicted rankings or KL divergence between output distributions). Cases with maximum committee disagreement will be selected for clinician annotation. The corrected rankings will be used to update all committee models via iterative fine-tuning. We will evaluate on tasks such as in-hospital mortality risk stratification and 30-day readmission prediction using MIMIC-IV data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, resource-efficient framework for integrating human expertise into clinical AI, directly reducing the annotation burden for model refinement. It will yield a publicly available active learning protocol tailored for EHR ranking tasks and demonstrate quantifiable gains in label efficiency compared to baseline methods on established clinical benchmarks.
proposals.novelty: While QBC is known in generic ML, its adaptation to refine ranking models on structured, temporal EHR data for clinical decision support is novel. This approach strategically leverages model committee disagreement, rather than simple prediction uncertainty, to guide expert feedback, a method not yet explored for clinical ranking problems.

proposals.title: CLIMATE: Contextualized Landscape Inference via Multi-modal Aggregation for Targeted Epidemiology
proposals.motivation: Respiratory virus surveillance is critical for public health but often lags due to reliance on confirmed test results, missing subclinical infections and testing biases. Furthermore, the efficacy of screening for a specific pathogen (e.g., a novel coronavirus) is confounded by the dynamic background prevalence of co-circulating viruses (e.g., influenza, RSV) which cause similar symptoms. A real-time, data-driven model of the viral landscape is needed to deconvolve these signals and optimize resource allocation.
proposals.hypothesis: A multi-modal fusion framework that creates unified, time-sensitive patient representations from EHRs and viral sequences can accurately infer a dynamic population-level viral prevalence landscape, and using this landscape to contextually parameterize screening models will improve their specificity and positive predictive value compared to static models.
proposals.method: We will extend the `meds_reader` library for efficient processing of longitudinal EHR data (clinical notes via a long-context language model, lab codes, vitals). A neural fusion architecture (e.g., cross-attention or late fusion) will integrate these modalities with viral genomic sequences (encoded via a pre-trained model) to output a patient state vector. These vectors will be aggregated via a temporal Hawkes process or recurrent network to model population-level viral dynamics. This dynamic landscape will then be used as a conditioning variable in a time-to-event model (inspired by Steinberg's prior work) for predicting target pathogen risk, optimizing screening thresholds.
proposals.contribution: 1) A novel multi-modal patient representation model for respiratory infection phenotyping. 2) A method for inferring real-time viral prevalence from heterogeneous patient data, not just confirmed tests. 3) A demonstration of context-aware screening where population dynamics directly inform individual-level risk assessment, closing the loop between surveillance and clinical decision support.
proposals.novelty: While multi-modal fusion and epidemic modeling exist separately, this work uniquely uses fused patient states—not just diagnostic labels—as the fundamental unit for landscape inference and directly feeds the inferred landscape back into adaptive, personalized screening algorithms, creating a synergistic loop.

proposals.title: Adversarial Noise Modeling for Discriminating Respiratory Pathogens from Noisy, Active Digital Phenotyping Data
proposals.motivation: Differentiating between respiratory pathogens (e.g., influenza, RSV, SARS-CoV-2) is critical for treatment and public health, but symptom self-reports are notoriously noisy and suffer from recall bias. Current machine learning models trained on electronic health records (EHRs) often treat these reports as ground truth, ignoring individual reporting variability and missingness, which limits their real-world clinical reliability. A method to explicitly model and separate this reporting noise from true disease progression is needed to build robust diagnostic classifiers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by using a probabilistic time-series model to disentangle latent disease progression from individual-specific reporting noise in active digital phenotyping data, we can identify more robust, pathogen-specific temporal signatures, leading to a classifier whose performance is less degraded by real-world data imperfections.
proposals.method: We will prospectively enroll participants with acute respiratory illness to provide 14 days of structured daily symptom reports, passive audio for cough detection, and wearable-derived physiology. The core analysis will use a variational autoencoder (VAE) with a structured latent space, where one component models the shared, pathogen-driven symptom trajectory and another models participant-specific reporting bias and noise. This model will be trained adversarially to ensure the trajectory component is invariant to the noise component. The inferred robust trajectories and their uncertainties will then be used as features in a gradient-boosted tree (XGBoost) or a simple neural network classifier for pathogen discrimination, validated against PCR-confirmed diagnoses.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel modeling framework that explicitly accounts for uncertainty in patient-reported outcomes for temporal clinical prediction. It will produce a validated classifier for respiratory pathogen discrimination based on robust digital phenotyping signatures. The methods and the open-source analysis pipeline (extending tools like `meds_reader`) will provide a foundation for noise-aware modeling of other longitudinal EHR and patient-generated health data tasks.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Steinberg et al. has focused on long-context models for EHRs and time-to-event pretraining, this proposal uniquely integrates an adversarial, probabilistic decomposition of noise and signal directly into the modeling of high-frequency digital phenotyping data for a fine-grained diagnostic task, moving beyond treating input data as clean labels.

proposals.title: Task-Specific EHR Summarization: Evaluating the Sufficiency of Focused Data Snippets for Clinical Prediction
proposals.motivation: Current clinical AI models often require processing a patient's entire longitudinal EHR, which is computationally expensive, raises privacy concerns, and is difficult to scale. Many common clinical tasks, however, may depend on a specific subset of the record. A systematic study is needed to determine when and how much data is truly necessary, enabling more efficient and deployable models.
proposals.hypothesis: For a defined set of common clinical prediction tasks, models trained on task-specific, temporally focused data snippets or structured summaries will achieve performance parity with models trained on the full, longitudinal EHR record.
proposals.method: We will leverage the MIMIC-IV and eICU-CRD datasets. Using the meds_reader library for efficient extraction, we will define multiple clinical tasks (e.g., in-hospital mortality, readmission, specific complication prediction). For each task, we will engineer focused data snippets (e.g., the last 24 hours of notes and labs) and structured summaries (e.g., problem lists, recent medication changes) as inputs. We will benchmark the performance of transformer-based models (e.g., ClinicalBERT, fine-tuned LLMs) trained on these focused inputs against a strong baseline model (inspired by Time-to-Event Pretraining methods) trained on the full longitudinal context.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide empirical evidence for the 'data necessity' of common clinical tasks, potentially reducing computational and data burdens. It will contribute a framework for creating task-specific EHR summaries and offer practical guidance for building efficient clinical prediction models that are easier to implement in resource-constrained settings.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (like Steinberg's 'Context Clues') evaluates long-context models, this proposal inverts the problem to deliberately restrict context. It systematically tests the sufficiency of minimalist, interpretable data representations against the prevailing paradigm of 'more data is always better' for EHR-based prediction.

proposals.title: MedAlign-Plus: Scaling and Diversifying a Clinical Instruction-Following Dataset for Robust Medical AI
proposals.motivation: Instruction-tuned large language models (LLMs) show promise for clinical applications, but their safety and utility are limited by the scale and diversity of their training data. Existing clinical instruction datasets like MedAlign are foundational but relatively small and lack broad specialty coverage, risking model bias and poor generalization to complex, real-world clinical tasks. Expanding such datasets is critical for developing reliable, multi-purpose clinical AI assistants.
proposals.hypothesis: Systematically expanding the MedAlign dataset with a larger volume of high-quality, clinician-authored instructions and reference responses across diverse medical specialties will significantly improve the clinical accuracy, safety, and task generalization of instruction-tuned LLMs compared to models trained on the original dataset.
proposals.method: We will leverage the established MedAlign framework and the meds_reader library for efficient EHR processing. A consortium of board-certified clinicians from 8+ specialties (e.g., oncology, psychiatry, surgery) will author new instruction-response pairs grounded in synthetic but realistic longitudinal EHR records, ensuring task diversity (diagnosis, planning, explanation). We will employ a structured data collection protocol with iterative clinician feedback and cross-specialty validation. The expanded dataset will be used to fine-tune a base clinical LLM (e.g., a model pretrained on EHRs or biomedical literature) using supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) techniques prevalent pre-2026.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce MedAlign-Plus, a significantly larger and more clinically diverse open-source instruction-tuning dataset, enabling more robust benchmarking of clinical LLMs. It will provide empirical evidence on how dataset scale and specialty diversity impact model performance on held-out clinical tasks and safety evaluations, guiding future dataset development.
proposals.novelty: While scaling datasets is common, this proposal focuses on systematic, specialty-driven diversification of clinical instructions, moving beyond breadth to structured depth. It uniquely combines Steinberg's expertise in clinical context modeling (from 'Context Clues') and efficient EHR processing (meds_reader) to create a scalable data generation pipeline.

proposals.title: CLINSCORE: Learning Clinician-Aligned Automated Metrics for Evaluating Large Language Models in Clinical Text Generation
proposals.motivation: Evaluating the output of clinical LLMs remains a major bottleneck. Current automated metrics like BLEU or ROUGE are known to correlate poorly with clinician judgments of factual accuracy, clinical relevance, and potential harm. This forces reliance on expensive and slow expert evaluation, hindering rapid model development and validation for safe clinical deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a set of automated metrics, trained to predict clinician rankings on diverse clinical text generation tasks, will achieve significantly higher correlation with expert judgment than existing general-purpose NLP metrics.
proposals.method: We will leverage Steinberg's 'meds_reader' library to process a large corpus of de-identified EHRs to construct a benchmark of clinical text generation tasks (e.g., patient summarization, care plan generation). We will collect clinician rankings (Likert-scale) on LLM outputs for these tasks. Using this dataset, we will train a multi-task regression model (e.g., based on a clinical BERT variant) to predict the clinician scores. The model's inputs will be the source context (e.g., patient records) and the LLM-generated text, enriched with task-specific features (e.g., medical concept overlap from UMLS, temporal consistency checks). The final metric suite, CLINSCORE, will be the ensemble of these learned predictors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the community with a validated, open-source automated evaluation suite that strongly aligns with clinical expertise, drastically reducing the cost of iterative LLM development for medicine. It will establish a new methodology for creating domain-specific evaluation metrics via learned alignment with expert judgment.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that adapts generic metrics, we propose to directly learn the evaluation function from clinician rankings on real clinical tasks. This approach integrates Steinberg's expertise in EHR context modeling and clinical prediction to create a task-aware, clinician-in-the-loop metric framework.

proposals.title: Benchmarking Domain-Specialization: A Rigorous Evaluation of Clinical LLMs on the MedAlign Framework
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of large language models (LLMs) in healthcare has led to a dichotomy between general-purpose models (e.g., GPT-4) and those fine-tuned on biomedical corpora (e.g., Med-PaLM, BioBERT). While domain-specific models promise superior clinical reasoning, their performance on standardized, multi-task benchmarks that reflect real-world clinical workflows remains inadequately characterized. The MedAlign benchmark, introduced in 2024, provides a crucial testbed for this evaluation, as it aggregates diverse clinical NLP tasks requiring complex inference over structured and unstructured electronic health record (EHR) data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that LLMs specifically fine-tuned on large-scale, domain-specific clinical data will significantly outperform general-purpose LLMs of comparable scale on the MedAlign benchmark, particularly on tasks requiring nuanced medical knowledge and temporal reasoning over patient records.
proposals.method: We will conduct a controlled benchmark study using the MedAlign framework, which includes tasks like clinical note generation, temporal reasoning, and medical question answering. We will evaluate leading clinical LLMs (e.g., Med-PaLM 2, ClinicalBERT, GatorTron) against general models (e.g., LLaMA-2, GPT-3.5) of similar parameter scale. Performance will be measured using MedAlign's established metrics (e.g., BLEU, ROUGE, accuracy, F1). We will employ Steinberg's 'meds_reader' library for efficient EHR data preprocessing and will analyze performance stratified by task type and required context length, extending methods from prior work on long-context evaluation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, apples-to-apples comparison of clinical versus general LLMs on the MedAlign benchmark, establishing a clear evidence base for the value of medical domain adaptation. It will yield actionable insights for the clinical NLP community on which model families and fine-tuning strategies are most effective for specific clinical task types, guiding future model development and deployment.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies evaluate models on isolated tasks, this proposal systematically leverages the integrated, multi-task MedAlign benchmark to dissect the specific capabilities—such as handling long clinical context and medical terminology—that domain-specific fine-tuning confers, moving beyond aggregate scores to a granular performance analysis.

proposals.title: Taxonomizing Clinical Intent: A Framework for Categorizing and Benchmarking EHR-Based Instruction Tasks for Large Language Models
proposals.motivation: The application of LLMs to Electronic Health Records (EHRs) is advancing, but progress is fragmented. Researchers and clinicians issue diverse, often ambiguous, natural language instructions (e.g., 'predict readmission,' 'summarize this patient for rounds,' 'identify drug interactions'), yet there is no systematic taxonomy to categorize these tasks. This lack of structure hinders the development of robust, generalizable models and makes it difficult to benchmark LLM performance across the true spectrum of clinical reasoning. A formal taxonomy is needed to map the space of EHR-based tasks, clarify their underlying demands on models, and establish standardized evaluation protocols.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that clinician instructions for EHR analysis can be decomposed into a finite set of core task types (e.g., information extraction, temporal reasoning, summarization, prediction) defined by their required operations on structured and unstructured data. Further, we hypothesize that the difficulty of these tasks for LLMs correlates predictably with taxonomic dimensions such as context length, need for temporal integration, and reasoning complexity.
proposals.method: We will first curate a large corpus of clinician instructions from real-world sources (clinical notes, research literature, and existing benchmark datasets like MIMIC-IV). Using a combination of expert clinician annotation and unsupervised topic modeling (e.g., BERTopic), we will iteratively develop a hierarchical taxonomy. We will then validate the taxonomy by mapping existing benchmarks (e.g., tasks from the 'Context Clues' paper) onto it and measuring LLM performance (using models like GPT-4, LLaMA-2, and clinical variants such as BioClinicalBERT) across taxonomic categories. Difficulty will be quantified using metrics like accuracy, F1 score, and a novel 'reasoning step' score derived from chain-of-thought prompting analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, empirically-grounded taxonomy of EHR-based instruction tasks, offering a common framework for the research community. It will produce a public dataset of categorized instructions and a clear mapping of which task characteristics challenge current LLMs. This will directly inform the design of more targeted training strategies (e.g., instruction tuning) and more meaningful, stratified benchmarking in clinical NLP.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (e.g., 'Context Clues') evaluates LLMs on specific tasks, no pre-2026 effort has systematically taxonomized the underlying *intent* and *operational requirements* of clinician instructions to create a general theory of EHR-based tasks. Our approach moves from ad-hoc benchmarking to a principled classification that links task structure to model performance.

proposals.title: MM-ClinicalInstruct: A Multi-Modal Instruction-Tuning Framework for Clinical Prediction from EHR Text and Medical Images
proposals.motivation: Electronic Health Records (EHRs) are inherently multi-modal, containing both structured/unstructured text and medical images (e.g., radiology, pathology). Current clinical foundation models and instruction-tuning efforts largely treat these modalities in isolation, creating a gap between how clinicians reason and how models are trained. A unified framework for instruction-following on multi-modal EHR data is crucial for developing more holistic and clinically useful AI assistants.
proposals.hypothesis: Instruction-tuning a multi-modal encoder (e.g., a Flan-T5 or LLaMA variant fused with a vision encoder like ViT) on a diverse set of tasks requiring joint reasoning over clinical text and images will yield a model that significantly outperforms unimodal or late-fusion baselines on complex clinical prediction and question-answering tasks.
proposals.method: We will construct a large-scale instruction dataset by pairing tasks from Steinberg's prior work (e.g., time-to-event, readmission) with relevant imaging studies (e.g., chest X-rays for heart failure prediction). We will use the meds_reader library for efficient text processing. The model architecture will be based on a pre-trained LLM (e.g., Flan-T5-XXL) and a pre-trained vision transformer (ViT). We will employ a cross-attention-based fusion mechanism (e.g., Perceiver-like resampler or LLaVA's projection layer) to integrate image features into the language model's context. Training will involve instruction-tuning on the multi-modal task mix, with careful evaluation on held-out clinical prediction benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) the first publicly described, large-scale instruction-tuning framework explicitly designed for joint EHR text and image tasks, 2) a new benchmark for multi-modal clinical instruction following, and 3) empirical evidence on the value of deep modality fusion versus simpler baselines for clinical AI.
proposals.novelty: While multi-modal medical AI exists, the explicit formulation of clinical prediction and reasoning as an instruction-following problem over fused text and images is novel. This moves beyond using images as mere additional input features to creating a model capable of executing diverse, composite instructions that require understanding both data types, aligning with Steinberg's focus on context and tasks.

proposals.title: Cohort-Thinker: Benchmarking and Training LLMs for Multi-Patient Clinical Cohort Analysis
proposals.motivation: Current clinical LLM research focuses on single-patient prediction, missing the crucial population-level reasoning needed for epidemiology, comparative effectiveness research, and hospital operations. Scaling to multi-patient analysis presents unique challenges in long-context modeling, cross-record relational reasoning, and synthesizing aggregate insights, which are underexplored. This gap limits the utility of LLMs for real-world public health and administrative decision support.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that large language models, when specifically trained and evaluated on tasks requiring explicit reasoning across hundreds of patient records, can generate accurate and clinically meaningful population-level insights that surpass simple aggregations of single-patient predictions.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel benchmark using the MIMIC-IV database, constructing tasks like identifying disease outbreak patterns, comparing treatment effectiveness across sub-cohorts, and predicting resource utilization trends. We will extend the meds_reader library to efficiently chunk and index records for cross-patient attention. Building on Steinberg's work on long-context evaluation, we will fine-tune a model like GPT-4 or LLaMA using a multi-stage curriculum: first on single-record tasks, then on paired records, and finally on large cohorts, explicitly training the model to generate structured cohort summaries and answer population-level queries.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first dedicated benchmark and methodology for multi-patient clinical LLM analysis, moving beyond single-patient modeling. It will contribute a new open-source framework for cohort-level EHR reasoning and demonstrate the feasibility and limitations of using LLMs for population-scale clinical insight generation.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that treats long patient contexts as a single extended sequence, this proposal explicitly architectures tasks and training to force relational reasoning *across* distinct patient records, treating the cohort as a set of interconnected documents rather than one monolithic text.

proposals.title: Federated Foundation Models for Healthcare: Adapting Small-Clinic Pretraining to Large Hospital EHRs
proposals.motivation: Foundation models for Electronic Health Records (EHRs) are typically pretrained on data from large, resource-rich academic medical centers, creating a performance and accessibility gap for smaller clinics. This work inverts the paradigm to explore if a model trained on aggregated data from diverse, smaller clinics—which represent a broader swath of patient populations and care settings—can be effectively adapted to a large hospital. This addresses a critical equity and scalability challenge in clinical AI by leveraging the more numerous small clinics as a potential data foundation.
proposals.hypothesis: A foundation model pretrained via federated learning on the longitudinal EHR data from a consortium of small, community-based clinics can, after targeted adaptation, match or exceed the performance of a model trained solely on large-hospital data for key clinical prediction tasks within the large hospital.
proposals.method: We will use a federated learning framework to pretrain a transformer-based model (e.g., based on the architecture from 'Context Clues') on de-identified EHR sequences from multiple small clinics, using the `meds_reader` library for efficient processing. This 'small-clinic foundation model' (SC-FM) will then be adapted to a large hospital's EHR system using several strategies: 1) continued pretraining on the target hospital's data, 2) multi-task fine-tuning on clinical tasks (e.g., readmission, mortality), and 3) context-aware prompting leveraging the long-context capabilities of the SC-FM. Performance will be benchmarked against a large-hospital-only pretrained model.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical evidence for the viability and value of a 'small-to-large' model sharing direction in medical AI. It will yield a novel adaptation framework for cross-institutional model transfer and contribute a publicly available, privacy-preserving pretraining methodology that could democratize access to high-performing clinical foundation models.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focuses on adapting large-hospital models to smaller settings, we explicitly invert this direction. The novelty lies in testing whether the data diversity and operational breadth of small clinics can produce a more robust and generalizable foundational representation than the deep but narrow data from a single large center.

proposals.title: Generalizability Under Scrutiny: A Multi-Site Benchmark for Evaluating EHR Foundation Models
proposals.motivation: Foundation models for Electronic Health Records (EHRs) promise to reduce the need for site-specific model development. However, their real-world utility depends on robust performance across diverse healthcare systems with varying data structures, coding practices, and patient populations. Current evaluations, often limited to a few large academic centers, fail to assess this critical adaptability, risking poor performance and hidden biases when deployed broadly.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the performance of a shared, pre-trained EHR foundation model will degrade significantly when applied to hospitals with divergent data characteristics, and that this degradation can be mitigated through targeted, lightweight adaptation strategies informed by measurable data shifts.
proposals.method: We will conduct a rigorous benchmark using Steinberg's meds_reader library to process longitudinal EHR data from 8-10 diverse hospital systems (e.g., community, rural, pediatric, different EHR vendors). We will evaluate a pre-trained transformer model (e.g., based on ClinicalBERT or a similar architecture) on core clinical prediction tasks (e.g., readmission, mortality) across all sites. Performance will be analyzed against quantitative measures of data shift (e.g., vocabulary divergence, temporal pattern differences). We will then test adaptation methods, including continued pre-training on target site data and prompt-tuning with task-specific context, to improve generalizability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic, multi-site benchmark for EHR foundation model generalizability, establishing concrete metrics for data shift in clinical settings. It will offer practical guidance on when and how to adapt shared models, moving the field beyond single-institution validation towards reliable, scalable clinical AI.
proposals.novelty: While prior work like 'Context Clues' evaluates model capabilities on long sequences, this study explicitly focuses on cross-institutional performance degradation as a core challenge, linking it to measurable data artifacts and testing pragmatic adaptation strategies derived from the foundation model paradigm.

proposals.title: Beyond Prediction: A Systematic Benchmark of Foundation Model Adaptability for EHR-Based Phenotyping and Outcome Discovery
proposals.motivation: Current evaluations of clinical foundation models, including Dr. Steinberg's work on long-context models, are heavily skewed towards a narrow set of supervised prediction tasks (e.g., mortality, readmission). This fails to capture the full spectrum of potential downstream applications in biomedical research, such as computational phenotyping for cohort discovery or unsupervised outcome identification, which are critical for precision medicine. A comprehensive assessment of model adaptability across these diverse task types is needed to understand their true utility and limitations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that foundation models pretrained on large-scale EHR data will demonstrate variable adaptability, excelling at supervised prediction tasks but showing significant performance degradation on unsupervised or semi-supervised tasks like phenotype clustering and novel outcome discovery, due to biases in their pretraining objectives and architectural constraints.
proposals.method: We will extend the evaluation framework from 'Context Clues' to a new benchmark suite of 15+ tasks, categorically grouped into supervised prediction, semi-supervised phenotyping (using methods like anchor-based learning and contrastive clustering), and unsupervised outcome discovery (using latent space probing and divergence measures). We will assess adaptability by fine-tuning and prompting (in-context learning) several state-of-the-art architectures (e.g., Transformer-based models like CLMBR and GatorTron) pretrained on EHRs. Performance will be measured using task-specific metrics (AUC, F1) and novel adaptability scores quantifying data efficiency and stability across tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic benchmark quantifying the adaptability of clinical foundation models beyond standard prediction, offering actionable insights for model selection and development for diverse research applications. It will produce a public benchmark suite to guide future model evaluation and identify specific architectural or pretraining weaknesses for tasks like phenotype discovery.
proposals.novelty: While prior work, including the PI's, focuses on model performance on known prediction tasks, this proposal uniquely shifts the focus to *adaptability* across a taxonomy of task types fundamental to biomedical research, introducing novel evaluation paradigms for unsupervised and semi-supervised clinical applications.

proposals.title: Departmental Foundation Models: Efficient, Specialized Pretraining for Clinical Specialty-Specific EHR Tasks
proposals.motivation: Generalist clinical foundation models, trained on heterogeneous hospital-wide data, are computationally expensive and may dilute domain-specific signal. For focused clinical decision support within a specialty (e.g., cardiology, oncology), a smaller model pretrained exclusively on relevant patient records could offer superior performance and efficiency. This addresses the practical need for deployable, high-accuracy models in resource-constrained clinical settings.
proposals.hypothesis: A transformer-based foundation model, pretrained exclusively on the longitudinal EHR data from a single clinical department, will outperform a generalist clinical foundation model of comparable size on department-specific prediction tasks, while requiring significantly less pretraining data and compute.
proposals.method: We will adapt the masked language modeling and time-to-event pretraining objectives from Steinberg's prior work to a department-specific corpus (e.g., all cardiology notes, labs, and medications from MIMIC-IV). Using the `meds_reader` library for efficient data extraction, we will pretrain a compact transformer (e.g., 100M parameter LLaMA or GPT-2 architecture) and compare it against a similarly-sized model pretrained on general hospital data. Evaluation will follow the 'Context Clues' framework, benchmarking on specialty-specific tasks like heart failure readmission or drug recommendation, using curated labels from department records.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic evidence for the 'scale-down' hypothesis in clinical AI, demonstrating that data quality and domain focus can trump sheer scale and heterogeneity. It will deliver a blueprint and open-source model for creating efficient, specialty-specific foundation models, lowering the barrier to entry for tailored clinical AI development.
proposals.novelty: While existing work scales up to generalist models, we explicitly investigate the opposite paradigm: intentional, data-efficient scaling down for specialization. The novelty lies in rigorously testing whether department-level pretraining, a natural clinical data boundary, is the optimal granularity for many practical applications.

proposals.title: From Prediction to Synthesis: Adapting a Clinical Foundation Model for Generative Tasks in Electronic Health Records
proposals.motivation: Current clinical foundation models are predominantly fine-tuned for discriminative tasks like mortality or readmission prediction. However, generative capabilities, such as creating realistic synthetic patient records for data augmentation or suggesting plausible future treatment pathways, remain underexplored. These capabilities are crucial for addressing data scarcity, privacy concerns, and supporting clinical decision support in a more interactive manner, all within the constraints of pre-2026 model architectures and EHR data formats.
proposals.hypothesis: A clinical foundation model, initially pretrained for time-to-event and other predictive tasks, can be effectively adapted for high-fidelity, conditional generation of longitudinal EHR sequences and treatment suggestions by employing targeted fine-tuning with next-token prediction and specific control mechanisms.
proposals.method: We will adapt a pre-trained clinical transformer model (e.g., based on Steinberg's prior work with long-context models and meds_reader). For synthetic record generation, we will fine-tune the model using a causal language modeling objective on structured EHR sequences, conditioning on patient demographics and initial diagnoses. For treatment pathway suggestion, we will employ a controlled text generation approach, using treatment codes as target labels within the sequence and leveraging reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) with clinician input to align suggestions with medical guidelines. Evaluation will use metrics like realism (via a discriminator model), clinical logic checks, and clinician review.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated framework for adapting predictive clinical FMs to generative tasks, expanding their utility beyond analytics. It will yield two concrete artifacts: a model capable of generating privacy-preserving synthetic EHR timelines for research and a prototype for interactive, context-aware treatment suggestion.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focused on predictive performance, this proposal systematically explores the adaptation of a single, pre-existing clinical FM for multiple distinct generative tasks, integrating control mechanisms and RLHF specifically for the structured, temporal nature of EHR data, rather than free-text medical notes.

proposals.title: The Trove Framework Under Scrutiny: A Multi-Institutional Validation Study for EHR-Based Clinical Prediction
proposals.motivation: The generalizability of machine learning models across different Electronic Health Record (EHR) systems remains a critical barrier to real-world clinical deployment. Models trained on data from a single institution often suffer performance degradation when applied elsewhere due to variations in data schema, coding practices, and patient populations. Validating the Trove framework, which integrates long-context modeling and efficient processing, across diverse institutions is a necessary step to assess its viability as a foundational tool for clinical AI.
