proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the Trove framework, leveraging its context-aware architecture and the meds_reader library for data harmonization, will demonstrate robust performance (e.g., within 5% AUC degradation) on core clinical prediction tasks when evaluated on EHR data from at least three external healthcare institutions not seen during its development.
proposals.method: We will apply the pre-trained Trove model, a transformer-based architecture optimized for long clinical sequences, to de-identified EHR datasets from three distinct healthcare networks. Using the meds_reader library, we will standardize raw EHR data (diagnoses, medications, lab values) into a unified temporal sequence format. We will then fine-tune and evaluate the model on held-out patient cohorts from each institution for tasks like mortality prediction and readmission, benchmarking against institution-specific models. Performance will be measured using area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUROC) and area under the precision-recall curve (AUPRC).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorous, multi-institutional validation of the Trove framework, establishing concrete evidence of its generalizability. It will produce a public benchmark of model performance across heterogeneous EHR systems, informing best practices for cross-institutional model deployment. The findings will directly address a key translational gap between model development and clinical application.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Steinberg et al. evaluated long-context models on clinical tasks, this study uniquely focuses on testing the integrated Trove *framework*—including its data processing pipeline and pre-trained model—as a cohesive system across truly external data sources, moving beyond single-database evaluations common in the field pre-2026.

proposals.title: MULTI-MODAL WEAK SUPERVISION: A UNIFIED FRAMEWORK FOR INTEGRATING STRUCTURED EHR AND CLINICAL NOTES
proposals.motivation: Clinical prediction models require large, high-quality labeled datasets, which are scarce and expensive to create in medicine. Weak supervision offers a solution by programmatically generating training labels, but existing frameworks (e.g., Snorkel) are typically applied to single data modalities, either structured EHR data or clinical text. Integrating these complementary sources—structured codes for specificity and clinical notes for rich context—within a unified weak supervision paradigm remains an open challenge, limiting the quality and scope of automated training sets.
proposals.hypothesis: A multi-modal weak supervision framework that jointly models labeling functions over structured EHR data and clinical notes will generate higher-quality training labels for downstream clinical prediction tasks than frameworks using either modality alone.
proposals.method: We will extend the Snorkel paradigm to a multi-modal setting. First, we will define separate sets of labeling functions (LFs) for structured data (e.g., rules on ICD codes, lab values) and clinical notes (e.g., keyword searches, simple NLP heuristics using the meds_reader library). These LFs will be integrated via a novel, modality-aware generative model that learns the accuracies and correlations of LFs both within and across modalities. The model will be implemented as a probabilistic graphical model, with inference performed via variational methods, outputting probabilistic training labels for a downstream discriminative model (e.g., a transformer fine-tuned on long clinical text context and structured feature vectors).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first generalizable framework for multi-modal weak supervision in clinical ML, enabling the creation of larger and more accurate training sets from heterogeneous EHR data. It will yield a publicly available software tool and benchmark results on tasks like readmission or mortality prediction, demonstrating significant performance gains over single-modality weak supervision baselines.
proposals.novelty: While multi-modal fusion and weak supervision are established separately, their combination for label generation is novel. The key innovation is the modality-aware generative model that explicitly accounts for the different statistical properties and reliabilities of labeling functions applied to tabular data versus unstructured text.

proposals.title: OntoGen: Rule-Based Synthetic Clinical Note Generation for Data-Efficient Foundation Model Training
proposals.motivation: Training robust clinical foundation models requires vast, diverse, and privacy-sensitive electronic health record (EHR) data, which is often inaccessible. Current synthetic data generation methods for EHRs, like GANs or language model fine-tuning, can produce unrealistic or inconsistent data and lack explicit control over clinical logic. A method to generate high-fidelity, logically coherent synthetic notes from a controlled rule set would address data scarcity and privacy while enabling targeted augmentation for rare conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a structured, ontology-driven generation system can produce synthetic clinical notes that are both linguistically realistic and clinically valid, and that models trained on this synthetic data will achieve performance comparable to models trained on real data for downstream prediction tasks.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage pipeline. First, we will formalize clinical knowledge from ontologies (e.g., SNOMED-CT) and rule sets (e.g., clinical guidelines) into a structured template and constraint system. Second, we will implement a controlled text generation model, likely a fine-tuned decoder (e.g., GPT-2/Flan-T5) or a rule-augmented Markov process, that populates templates with coherent text, ensuring temporal consistency and logical adherence to medical rules. The synthetic notes will be validated for clinical plausibility by experts and used to train a clinical prediction model (e.g., a BERT-style model via the meds_reader library), with performance benchmarked against a model trained on real, de-identified notes on tasks like readmission prediction.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel, controllable method for generating privacy-preserving synthetic clinical narratives, 2) a public framework and benchmark for evaluating the utility of synthetic EHR text, and 3) evidence on the feasibility of using rule-grounded synthetic data for training performant clinical NLP models, reducing dependency on large real-world datasets.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that generates EHR data at the code or tabular level, or uses language models in an uncontrolled manner, this approach inverts the typical analytics pipeline by explicitly using a formal clinical ontology and rule set as the primary source for controlled, explainable narrative generation.

proposals.title: INSPECT-Extended: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Pulmonary Embolism Outcome Prediction Using Longitudinal EHRs
proposals.motivation: The INSPECT dataset provides a rich, longitudinal resource for pulmonary embolism (PE) research, but existing benchmarks focus narrowly on diagnostic and short-term prognostic tasks. In clinical practice, PE management requires predicting a broader spectrum of outcomes, including long-term complications, treatment response, and recurrence risk. A systematic evaluation of modern long-context models on these diverse tasks is lacking, limiting our understanding of their real-world clinical utility.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that transformer-based models, particularly those pretrained for long-context understanding and time-to-event modeling, will demonstrate variable performance across distinct PE-related outcome tasks, revealing task-specific architectural and pretraining requirements for optimal EHR-based prediction.
proposals.method: We will extend the INSPECT benchmark by defining and curating labels for three new clinical prediction tasks: 1) 90-day major bleeding risk post-anticoagulation, 2) 1-year chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension (CTEPH) diagnosis, and 3) 6-month PE recurrence. Using the meds_reader library for efficient data extraction, we will evaluate a suite of models, including specialized long-context transformers (e.g., Longformer, BigBird) and time-to-event deep survival models, benchmarking them against traditional clinical scores and baseline RNNs. Performance will be assessed using time-dependent AUC and Brier scores, with rigorous cross-validation.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first comprehensive benchmark for multi-task PE outcome prediction from EHRs, providing a standardized evaluation framework for the community. It will deliver critical insights into which model architectures and pretraining strategies are best suited for specific clinical endpoints, guiding future model development for complex, longitudinal medical predictions.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has evaluated long-context models on EHRs, this study is novel in its focused application to a spectrum of under-studied, clinically critical PE outcomes within a single, well-characterized cohort, enabling a direct comparison of model capabilities across distinct prediction challenges.

proposals.title: Generalizability of Foundation Models for EHRs: A Multi-Institutional Validation Study of INSPECT-Trained Architectures
proposals.motivation: Foundation models trained on large-scale Electronic Health Record (EHR) datasets like INSPECT show promise for clinical prediction tasks. However, their real-world utility depends on generalizability across diverse healthcare systems with varying data structures, coding practices, and patient populations. Pre-2026, systematic external validation of such models is limited, creating a significant barrier to clinical trust and deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that foundation models pre-trained on the INSPECT dataset will demonstrate significant performance degradation on external, multi-institutional EHR data due to domain shift, but that task-specific fine-tuning with context-aware architectures can recover and even surpass the performance of locally-trained models.
proposals.method: We will select 2-3 state-of-the-art models (e.g., BEHRT, Med-BERT, or a clinical T5 variant) pre-trained on INSPECT. Using the meds_reader library for efficient processing, we will evaluate these models on held-out external datasets from distinct hospital networks (e.g., MIMIC-IV, All of Us, or a private partner EHR). Tasks will include time-to-event prediction and diagnosis classification. We will employ rigorous evaluation metrics (C-index, AUROC) and conduct error analysis to identify failure modes linked to institutional context, using methods from Steinberg's prior work on long-context model evaluation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive benchmark of INSPECT foundation models' cross-institutional generalizability, establishing a clear performance baseline for the field. It will produce a framework for auditing model robustness and a set of actionable recommendations for mitigating domain shift in clinical AI, directly informing safe translation to practice.
proposals.novelty: While model validation is standard, a focused, large-scale assessment of foundation model generalizability from a single large pre-training corpus (INSPECT) to multiple external EHRs is novel. The integration of context-analysis techniques from long-context evaluation to diagnose generalizability failures represents a new methodological synergy.

proposals.title: SynthEHR-Pretrain: A Framework for Privacy-Preserving Multimodal Pretraining on Synthetic Longitudinal Patient Records
proposals.motivation: Training robust foundation models for clinical prediction requires massive, multimodal datasets of longitudinal Electronic Health Records (EHRs), which are scarce due to privacy constraints and data silos. Existing pretraining methods, like time-to-event modeling, are data-hungry and limited by the scale and diversity of real-world cohorts. Synthetic data generation offers a path to scale, but current methods often fail to preserve critical long-range temporal dependencies and cross-modal relationships (e.g., lab trends, imaging findings, and medication changes) necessary for clinical reasoning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a generative model trained to produce high-fidelity, privacy-guaranteed synthetic multimodal patient trajectories can create a scalable pretraining dataset that, when used to pretrain a transformer-based clinical predictor, will match or exceed the downstream task performance of models pretrained on real data alone, while providing formal privacy assurances.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage framework. First, we will train a conditional diffusion model or a GAN with differential privacy (DP) guarantees on a real, de-identified multimodal EHR dataset (structured codes, clinical notes, time-series). The model will be conditioned on patient demographics and generate coherent longitudinal sequences of events. Second, we will use this synthetic corpus to pretrain a transformer encoder (e.g., a Longformer variant) using a masked prediction objective on structured data and a contrastive loss aligning clinical notes with their corresponding temporal context. Performance will be evaluated on held-out real data for tasks like readmission prediction and phenotype classification, benchmarking against models pretrained only on real data.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) an open-source framework for generating privacy-preserving, multimodal synthetic EHRs with formal DP guarantees, 2) a large-scale synthetic pretraining dataset for the research community, and 3) empirical evidence on the utility of synthetic data for scaling clinical foundation model pretraining, directly addressing data scarcity and privacy bottlenecks.
proposals.novelty: While synthetic EHR generation exists, its application for scaling *multimodal* and *long-context* pretraining is novel. Our approach uniquely integrates DP-SGD training of the generator with a pretraining strategy specifically designed for the long, heterogeneous sequences it produces, building directly on Steinberg's work in long-context evaluation and time-to-event modeling.

proposals.title: Generative Imaging from EHRs: A Conditional Diffusion Model for Predicting CT Pulmonary Angiography Findings in Suspected Pulmonary Embolism
proposals.motivation: CT Pulmonary Angiography (CTPA) is the gold standard for diagnosing pulmonary embolism (PE), but it exposes patients to radiation and contrast. A method to predict likely imaging findings from the EHR could help prioritize high-risk patients and reduce unnecessary scans. This addresses a critical inverse problem in clinical AI: moving from diagnostic suspicion back to probable imaging phenotype.
proposals.hypothesis: Conditional generative models, trained on paired EHR and imaging data, can synthesize realistic CTPA image patches that capture the specific vascular findings (e.g., filling defects) associated with a patient's clinical context and suspected PE diagnosis.
proposals.method: We will leverage the MIMIC-IV and MIMIC-CXR-JPG datasets, linking EHRs to CTPA studies. Using the meds_reader library for efficient EHR processing, we will encode longitudinal clinical data (vitals, labs, notes) into a context vector via a transformer encoder. This vector will condition a latent diffusion model (LDM) trained to reconstruct and generate 3D CTPA patches. The model will be trained using a task-specific objective that combines image reconstruction loss with a classification loss on known PE labels derived from radiology reports.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel generative framework for 'imaging from EHRs,' extending beyond classification to synthetic data generation. It will provide a proof-of-concept for using AI to visualize probable disease manifestations, potentially aiding clinical decision-support and educational simulation. The method and trained model will be released as an open-source tool.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (e.g., Steinberg's 'Time-to-Event Pretraining') uses EHRs for prognostic tasks, this inverts the paradigm to generate diagnostic imaging data. It combines the team's expertise in long-context EHR modeling with state-of-the-art generative models (diffusion) for a novel clinical synthesis task not explored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Robust Clinical Prediction via Debiasing Copy-Pasted Diagnoses in Longitudinal EHR Sequences
proposals.motivation: Electronic Health Records (EHRs) are rife with 'copy-forward' artifacts, where diagnoses are artificially repeated across visits for billing or documentation continuity, creating spurious temporal patterns. This artificial repetition can mislead modern long-context foundation models, which are trained to extract predictive signals from sequence order and frequency, leading to inflated and non-robust performance on clinical prediction tasks. Addressing this is a critical step towards deploying reliable models in real-world clinical settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling and mitigating the influence of copy-forwarded diagnostic tokens within EHR sequences will significantly improve the robustness and generalization of clinical prediction models, leading to performance that better reflects true patient state rather than documentation artifacts.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage training framework. First, we will train a lightweight auxiliary model (e.g., a transformer encoder) on synthetic and real EHR data to detect and label copy-forwarded diagnosis tokens, using patterns like exact repetition without intervening clinical events. Second, we will integrate this signal into a primary clinical prediction model (e.g., a model from Steinberg's 'Context Clues' work) via attention masking or token-weighting mechanisms that down-weight the influence of identified copy-forward tokens during representation learning. We will evaluate robustness on held-out data and on synthetically corrupted sequences from MIMIC-IV.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel method for debiasing a pervasive, realistic noise source in EHR data, 2) a framework for integrating artifact detection directly into clinical prediction model training, and 3) new benchmarks and analysis for evaluating model robustness to documentation practices, advancing the field towards more trustworthy clinical AI.
proposals.novelty: While existing work focuses on handling missing data or noise, explicitly modeling and mitigating the systematic bias introduced by clinical documentation practices like copy-forwarding is novel. Our approach moves beyond simple sequence modeling to incorporate a model of the documentation process itself.

proposals.title: Temporal Attention Rebalancing: Mitigating Performance Decay in Long-Context EHR Models for Complex, Late-Stage Disease Tokens
proposals.motivation: Electronic Health Record (EHR) sequences for chronic diseases grow longer and more complex over time, with later tokens often containing critical information about disease progression and complications. Recent work, including Steinberg's 'Context Clues', shows that long-context models (e.g., Transformer-based architectures) suffer from performance decay on tokens appearing later in lengthy sequences, a critical flaw for clinical prediction. This decay creates a dangerous negative correlation where model accuracy is lowest precisely when patient complexity and need for accurate forecasting are highest.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that performance decay on later, complex EHR tokens is caused by a combination of attention dilution and insufficient modeling of temporal disease progression dynamics. A method that explicitly rebalances attention and incorporates a learned temporal prior will significantly mitigate this decay.
proposals.method: We will extend a pre-trained long-context Transformer (e.g., Longformer or a Mamba-based architecture) using two novel components. First, a **Temporal Importance Weighting** module will apply a learnable, monotonic weighting function to the attention scores, up-weighting contributions from later tokens based on a learned 'complexity signal'. Second, we will integrate a **Time-to-Event Auxiliary Task** (inspired by Steinberg's prior imaging work) during fine-tuning, where the model must predict the time to a future clinical event from any token, forcing it to maintain predictive utility across the entire sequence. We will train and evaluate on large-scale, longitudinal EHR datasets (e.g., MIMIC-IV) using time-stratified evaluation to measure performance on early vs. late sequence segments.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, architecturally-grounded solution to a key failure mode of long-context models in clinical AI. It will deliver a new state-of-the-art method for modeling progressive disease in EHRs and offer empirical insights into the relationship between sequence position, information density, and model attention in temporal data.
proposals.novelty: While existing methods address general long-context decay via sparse attention, our approach is novel in its clinical specificity, targeting the *correlation* between token position and disease complexity. The integration of a time-to-event auxiliary task for sequence model calibration is a unique adaptation from medical imaging to the temporal EHR domain.

proposals.title: ClinicalBERT-Scissors: Identifying and Modeling Predictive Short Sequences in Longitudinal EHRs
proposals.motivation: While long-context models for Electronic Health Records (EHRs) show promise, their computational cost and data hunger limit clinical deployment. Furthermore, long patient histories contain redundant or irrelevant information, diluting predictive signal. A method to automatically isolate the most informative, short subsequences could enable efficient, robust, and interpretable clinical prediction models.
proposals.hypothesis: A small set of temporally localized, short event sequences within a long EHR can be identified that are as predictive for key clinical outcomes as the entire patient history, and modeling these sequences directly will improve efficiency and performance over whole-history approaches.
proposals.method: We will leverage Steinberg's 'meds_reader' library to process MIMIC-IV data. Using a pre-trained clinical BERT model as a feature extractor, we will apply a differentiable top-k selection mechanism (e.g., a gumbel-softmax or reinforcement learning-based selector) over temporally encoded event windows to identify critical subsequences. These selected short sequences will then be fed into a lightweight transformer or LSTM for downstream prediction tasks like mortality or readmission, with the selector trained end-to-end using task-specific labels.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for data-efficient EHR modeling that reduces computational overhead. It will yield interpretable insights into which specific clinical episodes are most predictive for a given outcome, moving beyond black-box long-context models. The resulting 'ClinicalBERT-Scissors' toolkit will be released for the community.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work on long-context modeling or simple attention mechanisms, this proposal explicitly learns to extract and model only a sparse set of short, high-value subsequences in an end-to-end fashion, framing EHR modeling as a sequence selection problem rather than a full-sequence compression problem.

proposals.title: Dual: Retrospective Inference of Unrecorded Early Clinical Events from Longitudinal EHR Context
proposals.motivation: Electronic Health Records (EHRs) are temporally fragmented, with early events often missing due to care transitions or incomplete documentation. This data gap hinders the development of accurate longitudinal models for disease progression and complicates causal inference. Inferring these missing early states from a patient's later, richer EHR context could significantly improve the utility of real-world data for clinical prediction and retrospective analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model trained on the complex, later-state context of a patient's EHR can accurately reconstruct specific, unrecorded early clinical events (e.g., initial symptoms, early medication trials, or past diagnoses) that are foundational to their current health state.
proposals.method: We will leverage Steinberg's 'meds_reader' library to process longitudinal EHR sequences from a large-scale dataset like MIMIC-IV. We will formulate the task as a masked event prediction problem, where early segments of a patient's timeline are artificially masked. A transformer-based architecture, inspired by the long-context evaluation in 'Context Clues', will be trained to predict these masked early events using the full, unmasked later context (e.g., subsequent diagnoses, lab trends, medications). The training objective will combine a cross-entropy loss for categorical events (diagnoses, procedures) with a regression loss for continuous variables, adapting the time-to-event pretraining concept for retrospective inference.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel 'retrospective imputation' paradigm for EHR analysis, moving beyond forward prediction. It will provide a method to probabilistically enrich incomplete patient histories, directly benefiting downstream tasks like phenotyping and risk stratification. The resulting model and framework will be a new tool for improving data quality and temporal understanding in clinical AI.
proposals.novelty: While most EHR models predict future states, this work explicitly inverts the temporal direction to infer the past, a non-trivial task requiring the model to disentangle and reason backward from complex, later-stage clinical evidence. It uniquely combines long-context modeling with a novel retrospective training objective tailored for clinical event sequences.

proposals.title: CLIN-EVAL: A Clinically-Grounded Benchmark for Evaluating Foundation Models on Electronic Health Records
proposals.motivation: The rapid proliferation of clinical foundation models (FMs) trained on EHR data has outpaced the development of robust, clinically-meaningful evaluation frameworks. Current benchmarks, often adapted from NLP, prioritize generic metrics like accuracy or AUROC, failing to capture critical healthcare dimensions such as clinical utility, temporal reasoning, and robustness to real-world data shifts. This gap hinders the reliable comparison of models and their translation into safe, effective clinical decision support tools.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-dimensional evaluation framework, incorporating clinical utility scores, temporal consistency metrics, and robustness assessments on curated, task-specific EHR datasets, will reveal significant performance disparities among state-of-the-art FMs that are not apparent using standard machine learning metrics alone.
proposals.method: We will construct CLIN-EVAL by: 1) Curating a suite of tasks from MIMIC-IV and other pre-2026 public EHR datasets, spanning prediction (e.g., readmission, mortality), information extraction, and temporal reasoning (e.g., lab trend forecasting). 2) Defining novel metrics, including a Clinical Utility Score (CUS) that weights predictions by clinical actionability and severity, and a Temporal Consistency Score (TCS) to evaluate model stability across sequential patient snapshots. 3) Systematically evaluating established FMs (e.g., ClinicalBERT, GatorTron, and our own long-context models from prior work) using this framework, comparing metric outcomes to standard AUROC/F1 baselines. 4) Validating framework outputs through structured feedback from clinical collaborators.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the community with a standardized, open-source benchmark for rigorous, clinically-informed evaluation of EHR-based FMs. It will yield a critical analysis of current model capabilities and limitations, directly guiding future model development toward clinically-relevant improvements. The framework will serve as a foundational tool for safer and more effective translational research in clinical AI.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has created EHR tasks, CLIN-EVAL's novelty lies in its systematic integration of domain-specific, clinically-derived evaluation metrics—beyond standard performance measures—into a unified benchmark, explicitly designed to assess the practical readiness of FMs for healthcare deployment.

proposals.title: The REAL-BENCH Framework: A Rigorous Evaluation of Clinical Foundation Models on Real-World Patient Care and Operational Tasks
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of clinical foundation models (CFMs) trained on electronic health records (EHRs) has outpaced systematic evaluation of their real-world utility. Current benchmarks often focus on narrow, proxy tasks (e.g., next-visit prediction) that do not directly translate to clinical decision-making or hospital operations, creating a gap between reported performance and practical impact. A standardized, multi-faceted benchmark grounded in tasks that matter to clinicians and administrators is urgently needed to guide model selection and development.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that current CFMs will exhibit significant performance variability across different real-world clinical task types (diagnostic, prognostic, operational), and that no single model architecture or pretraining objective will dominate across all categories, revealing critical task-specific strengths and weaknesses.
proposals.method: We will develop the REAL-BENCH framework, curating a suite of tasks from MIMIC-IV and a real-world institutional EHR: 1) Diagnostic (e.g., phenotyping from longitudinal notes), 2) Prognostic (e.g., time-to-event prediction for readmission), and 3) Operational (e.g., length-of-stay forecasting). We will benchmark diverse pre-2026 CFMs, including transformer-based models (e.g., ClinicalBERT, GatorTron) and time-series architectures (e.g., RNNs, TCNs), using the `meds_reader` library for standardized data extraction. Evaluation will rigorously assess performance, robustness to missing data, and computational efficiency, extending methods from Steinberg's prior work on long-context evaluation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, publicly available benchmark for CFMs focused on actionable clinical and operational outcomes, moving beyond academic proxies. It will deliver a clear taxonomy of model strengths, offering practitioners evidence-based guidance for model selection and identifying critical gaps for future research in clinical AI.
proposals.novelty: Unlike existing benchmarks, REAL-BENCH explicitly ties model evaluation to distinct stakeholder-valued task categories (clinical vs. operational) and emphasizes time-to-event and long-context prediction, areas highlighted in Steinberg's recent work but not yet synthesized into a holistic evaluation standard.

proposals.title: CLIN-FM-TAX: A Standardized Taxonomy for Architectures and Training of Clinical Foundation Models
proposals.motivation: The rapid proliferation of clinical foundation models (CFMs) for EHRs, imaging, and multimodal data has created a fragmented landscape. Without a common framework, comparing model performance, understanding architectural trade-offs, and guiding future development is severely hampered. A standardized taxonomy is urgently needed to unify terminology and enable systematic evaluation across tasks like long-context prediction and time-to-event analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-axis taxonomy, categorizing CFMs by architectural backbone, modality integration, and training paradigm, will reveal consistent relationships between design choices and performance on core clinical tasks. This will provide a predictive framework for model selection and development.
proposals.method: We will first conduct a systematic literature review of CFMs published pre-2026, extracting architectural details (e.g., transformer variants, CNN-Transformer hybrids), training approaches (e.g., pretraining objectives like masked language modeling, contrastive learning, time-to-event), and data modalities. We will then define orthogonal taxonomy axes and validate them by applying the taxonomy to a curated benchmark of existing models (e.g., those evaluated in 'Context Clues' and 'Time-to-Event Pretraining'). Statistical analysis will correlate taxonomy categories with performance on tasks such as mortality prediction and readmission.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first community-standard taxonomy for CFMs, enabling clear communication and comparison. It will yield a public, interactive classification tool and a set of evidence-based guidelines linking architectural choices to clinical task performance, directly informing future model design.
proposals.novelty: While taxonomies exist for general AI, none address the unique constraints and objectives of clinical AI, such as handling longitudinal records, integrating multimodal data, and optimizing for risk prediction. This taxonomy is grounded in the specific methodologies and evaluation tasks of the clinical domain.

proposals.title: Red-Teaming Clinical Foundation Models: A Systematic Framework for Adversarial Safety and Bias Evaluation
proposals.motivation: Clinical foundation models (CFMs) trained on large-scale electronic health records (EHRs) are being rapidly developed for diagnostic and prognostic tasks. However, their deployment carries significant risks of propagating harmful biases and making unsafe predictions, which are difficult to detect with standard evaluation on held-out test sets. A systematic, adversarial probing methodology is urgently needed to uncover these failure modes before real-world clinical integration.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying structured red-teaming techniques—inspired by AI safety research—to clinical foundation models will reveal critical, previously undetected vulnerabilities related to demographic biases, temporal inconsistencies, and context-dependent safety failures that standard performance metrics miss.
proposals.method: We will develop a clinical red-teaming framework that generates adversarial EHR sequences. Building on Steinberg's 'meds_reader' and context evaluation work, we will create a perturbation library targeting structured data (e.g., lab values, medications) and clinical text. We will use gradient-based methods (e.g., Projected Gradient Descent) and discrete token attacks to craft inputs that probe for biased predictions across protected attributes. The framework will be tested on CFMs adapted from architectures like CLMBR or G-BERT, evaluating their robustness on tasks such as mortality prediction and readmission.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first dedicated framework for adversarial safety evaluation of CFMs, producing a benchmark suite of failure cases. It will contribute a new evaluation paradigm that moves beyond accuracy to assess model trustworthiness, offering actionable insights for developers to harden models against hazardous failures.
proposals.novelty: While red-teaming is established in NLP and vision safety, its systematic application to the structured, longitudinal, and high-stakes domain of clinical EHR models is novel. This approach fundamentally shifts the evaluation goal from measuring performance to proactively searching for catastrophic failures.

proposals.title: Circuit Analysis of Clinical Foundation Models: Mechanistic Interpretability for EHR-Based Predictions
proposals.motivation: Clinical foundation models (CFMs) trained on electronic health records (EHRs) show strong predictive performance but operate as opaque 'black boxes,' limiting clinical trust and utility. Mechanistic interpretability, which aims to reverse-engineer model circuits, has advanced in NLP but remains largely unexplored in the complex, multimodal, and temporally structured domain of clinical data. Understanding the specific computational subgraphs and feature representations CFMs use for tasks like mortality or readmission prediction is crucial for validating their safety and uncovering potential biases.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying circuit-based mechanistic interpretability methods to clinical foundation models will reveal interpretable, medically plausible sub-networks (circuits) that correspond to known clinical syndromes or decision pathways, and that these circuits will be more causally faithful to model decisions than standard post-hoc attribution methods.
proposals.method: We will adapt and extend methods from NLP mechanistic interpretability (e.g., activation patching, path patching, and dictionary learning for sparse autoencoders) to a transformer-based clinical foundation model pre-trained on longitudinal EHR sequences. Using Steinberg's 'meds_reader' library for efficient data processing, we will first train a model on MIMIC-IV data for a specific clinical prediction task. We will then systematically intervene on model activations to identify minimal sufficient circuits for given predictions. The analysis will focus on how the model attends to and combines specific clinical codes (diagnoses, medications) across the patient timeline to form its internal representations.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce one of the first frameworks for applying rigorous mechanistic interpretability to clinical AI, moving beyond correlation to causal model understanding. It will yield concrete, auditable circuit descriptions for high-stakes predictions, providing a new tool for model validation, bias detection, and potentially novel clinical insight discovery. The developed software tools will be released to the community.
proposals.novelty: While mechanistic interpretability is nascent in NLP, its application to the structured, high-stakes domain of clinical foundation models is novel. This proposal uniquely combines Steinberg's expertise in clinical model evaluation (Context Clues) and efficient EHR processing (meds_reader) with cutting-edge, causality-focused interpretability methods not yet standard in medical AI.

proposals.title: RAG-CFM: A Retrieval-Augmented Generation Framework for Verifiable Clinical Foundation Models
proposals.motivation: Clinical foundation models (CFMs) trained on vast electronic health record (EHR) data can memorize spurious correlations, leading to uninterpretable and potentially unsafe predictions. Current methods lack a systematic mechanism to ground model inferences in retrievable, verifiable medical knowledge from trusted sources like clinical guidelines or medical literature. This limits clinical utility and trust, especially for complex, low-frequency conditions where model confidence may be misplaced.
proposals.hypothesis: Augmenting a clinical foundation model with a dynamic retrieval mechanism over a curated medical knowledge base will improve prediction accuracy, calibration, and interpretability on complex clinical tasks compared to the base CFM alone.
proposals.method: We will adapt the RETRO or FiD architecture for clinical data. Using the meds_reader library, we will process longitudinal EHR sequences from MIMIC-IV into patient context windows. For each prediction task (e.g., time-to-event for heart failure), a dense retriever (e.g., Contriever) will query a knowledge base of PubMed abstracts and guideline snippets. Retrieved passages will be concatenated with the patient context and fed into a pretrained CFM (e.g., based on Steinberg's time-to-event pretraining) for final prediction. We will evaluate on held-out test sets from MIMIC-IV.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, open-source framework for knowledge-grounded clinical prediction, demonstrating measurable gains in performance on challenging tasks. It will provide a template for making CFM predictions more transparent by explicitly linking them to supporting evidence, a critical step towards auditability and safe deployment.
proposals.novelty: While RAG is established in NLP, its integration with CFMs for core prediction tasks (not just QA) is unexplored. This proposal uniquely combines Steinberg's expertise in long-context EHR modeling and time-to-event pretraining with a retrieval paradigm tailored for clinical evidence, moving beyond simple fine-tuning on labeled records.

proposals.title: DiverseEHR: A Data-Centric Framework for Improving Clinical Foundation Model Robustness via Multi-Source, Multi-Population Pretraining
proposals.motivation: Current clinical foundation models are often trained on data from a limited set of large, well-resourced healthcare systems, leading to performance disparities and poor generalization across diverse patient populations and care settings. This data homogeneity, coupled with a predominant focus on scaling model parameters, risks perpetuating algorithmic bias and limits real-world clinical utility. Addressing this requires a fundamental shift towards scaling data diversity as a primary lever for model improvement.
proposals.hypothesis: A clinical foundation model of fixed, moderate size, pretrained on a massively diverse corpus of Electronic Health Records (EHRs) spanning varied demographics, geographies, and healthcare delivery systems, will demonstrate superior robustness, fairness, and out-of-distribution generalization compared to larger models trained on less diverse data.
proposals.method: We will construct DiverseEHR, a multi-source dataset integrating structured EHRs and clinical notes from over 50 global partners, curated via the meds_reader library for harmonization. Using a fixed Transformer encoder architecture (e.g., 250M parameters), we will pretrain with a multi-task objective combining masked language modeling on clinical text and a novel 'context-aware' time-to-event prediction task on structured records. We will rigorously evaluate on held-out populations and specialized clinical prediction tasks, benchmarking against larger models (e.g., GPT-3 scale) trained on narrower data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a scalable, privacy-aware framework for aggregating diverse clinical data; 2) empirical evidence that data diversity, not just model scale, is critical for robust clinical AI; and 3) a publicly benchmarked model demonstrating improved fairness across demographic subgroups. It will shift the field's focus towards data-centric scaling strategies.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (e.g., Steinberg's 'Context Clues') evaluates long-context models on existing EHRs, this proposal actively engineers training data diversity as an independent variable. It challenges the prevailing 'scale-is-all' paradigm in clinical AI by holding model size constant while systematically varying data composition, a controlled experiment not yet conducted at this scale.

ideas.title: Adaptive Query-by-Committee for Clinical Ranking Refinement
ideas.description: This idea adapts the 'Query-by-Committee' active learning paradigm, commonly used in fields like geological survey for efficient resource exploration, to the problem of integrating clinician feedback to improve a medical AI's ranking model. In the analogous solution, multiple predictive models (a committee) guide where to sample next by targeting areas of maximum disagreement, thereby optimizing expert effort for knowledge gain. Adapted here, an ensemble of clinical ranking models would identify patient cases or decision points where their outputs diverge significantly. These high-disagreement, high-uncertainty cases are then prioritized for clinician review and correction. The expert's feedback on the correct ranking or diagnosis is integrated back into the training process for all models in the ensemble, systematically refining the committee's collective judgment and improving the system's overall ranking accuracy with minimal expert queries.

ideas.title: Noise-Robust Modeling of Symptom Trajectories Using Active Digital Phenotyping for Respiratory Pathogen Discrimination
ideas.description: This study designs a system to capture high-fidelity temporal symptom data while explicitly modeling reporting uncertainty. Participants with acute respiratory illness are enrolled prospectively and report symptoms daily via a structured digital diary for 14 days, supplemented by passive audio sensing for cough and wearable data for physiological trends. The analysis uses probabilistic time-series models that separate true disease progression patterns from individual reporting noise and missing data, identifying robust, pathogen-specific temporal signatures. The final classifier incorporates both the inferred progression features and their associated uncertainties, ensuring the model is reliable when applied to real-world clinical data with imperfect recall.

ideas.title: Active Learning for Discovery and Validation of Novel Gene-Disease Associations
ideas.description: This system integrates a predictive model for novel gene-disease associations with an active learning framework that strategically queries clinicians. The model generates candidate links by analyzing genomic and phenotypic patterns not yet documented in the literature. The active learning loop then presents the most uncertain or high-impact predictions to experts for validation or correction, using this feedback to retrain the model, improve its ranking of future candidates, and refine the underlying discovery criteria, thereby accelerating the translation of genomic signals into validated, expert-informed hypotheses.

ideas.title: Dynamic Viral Landscape Modeling via Multi-Modal Patient State Fusion
ideas.description: This research direction develops a framework that uses multi-modal fusion techniques—integrating clinical notes, lab codes, viral genomic sequences, and non-clinical data streams—to create a unified, time-sensitive representation of a patient's respiratory infection state. These individual patient representations are then aggregated to infer, in near real-time, the dynamic mix and prevalence of co-circulating viruses within a population. This inferred, context-aware viral landscape model is subsequently used to dynamically parameterize and optimize screening efficacy for target pathogens, whose signals are often confounded by the current background of other infections. The synergy lies in closing the loop: multi-modal data fusion enables accurate, granular patient phenotyping, which feeds the dynamic landscape model, which in turn provides the essential context to make individual screening decisions more robust and adaptive.

ideas.title: Fusing continuous digital biomarkers with point-of-care test results for dynamic infection risk screening
ideas.description: This study evaluates a hybrid screening strategy that integrates objective, continuous physiological data from wearable devices with discrete rapid antigen test results. A machine learning model is trained to identify deviations from an individual's physiological baseline that are associated with infection, creating a digital symptom score. This continuous risk estimate is formally combined with the binary test outcome using a Bayesian framework to produce a dynamic, personalized probability of infection. The system is validated for its ability to improve early detection, particularly in pre-symptomatic or pauci-symptomatic cases, compared to strategies relying on self-reported symptoms alone.

ideas.title: Causal Invariant Learning from Multi-Source Digital Phenotypes for Generalizable Symptom Detection
ideas.description: This research designs a system that moves beyond simple correlation of sensor data with symptoms. It applies causal structure learning to time-series wearable and digital phenotyping data to distinguish true physiological precursors from spurious, context-dependent correlates. The core innovation is an adversarial neural network architecture that is explicitly trained to discard features predictive of the data source (e.g., device model, user age group, or environment) while preserving features predictive of the clinical symptom. This results in digital biomarkers that are invariant to common confounders, enabling the development of screening tools that maintain accuracy and specificity when deployed in new, heterogeneous populations with diverse devices and lifestyles.

ideas.title: Digital Phenotype Gap Mining: Using Passive Sensor Data to Identify and Validate Unsupported Clinical Phenotypes
ideas.description: This research direction develops a system that mines biomedical literature to flag putative clinical phenotypes or symptom clusters that lack strong genetic or mechanistic support. Crucially, it then queries large-scale, real-world digital phenotyping datasets—from wearables, smartphones, and EHR-integrated sensors—to search for objective, behavioral, or physiological signatures correlating with these unsupported phenotypes. This allows the system to move beyond identifying a mere publication gap to proposing a 'data-supported gap,' where sensor-derived evidence suggests the phenotype is real and measurable, thereby prioritizing the most tractable research opportunities. The final output is a ranked list of high-priority, sensor-validated phenotypic gaps, each paired with candidate digital biomarkers, ready for targeted genetic or interventional studies.

ideas.title: Context-Aware, Uncertainty-Modeling Phenotype Normalization via Integrated Patient Graphs and Neuro-Symbolic Reasoning
ideas.description: This system moves beyond simple mapping to perform robust phenotype normalization by constructing a dynamic patient knowledge graph from the entire electronic health record, integrating temporal sequences of clinical events. A neuro-symbolic architecture uses a clinical language model to generate candidate phenotype concepts from notes, then reasons over the patient graph and ontology hierarchies with a probabilistic symbolic module to resolve ambiguities and contextualize findings. The output is a set of ranked, justified normalized concepts with associated confidence metrics, enabling downstream applications to explicitly account for uncertainty and clinical context in their analyses.

ideas.title: A Self-Improving Biomedical Knowledge Engine via Continuous NLP-Driven Literature Mining
ideas.description: This research fuses continuous literature monitoring with advanced biomedical NLP to create an autonomous, self-updating knowledge system. The system uses BioBERT-like models not just for static extraction, but to actively scan and analyze incoming publications, identifying novel relationships and emerging evidence patterns. These insights are then used to intelligently prioritize and guide the periodic retraining of the core model (AMELIE), ensuring updates are focused on the most significant new knowledge. This creates a virtuous cycle where improved extraction directly fuels more efficient and relevant model maintenance, transforming it from a passive updater into an active, learning knowledge engine.

ideas.title: A Federated, Schema-Agnostic Pipeline for Heterogeneous Biomedical Literature Ingestion
ideas.description: This research seed expands literature parsing beyond PubMed abstracts by drawing an analogy from astronomy's solution to integrating heterogeneous data sources. In astronomy, the Virtual Observatory (VO) framework solved the parallel problem of federating access to disparate, globally distributed data archives (from different telescopes, sky surveys, and simulations) by implementing standard data models (e.g., VOTable) and query protocols (e.g., SIA, SSA). This allowed for unified discovery and analysis without forcing a single, central database. Adapting this solution, the proposed research would develop a federated, schema-agnostic pipeline for biomedical literature. The core idea is to create a lightweight, domain-specific protocol and a common intermediate representation—a 'Biomedical Literature Object Model'—that can ingest and semantically normalize documents from diverse sources like preprint servers (e.g., bioRxiv, medRxiv), clinical trial registries (ClinicalTrials.gov), regulatory reports (FDA, EMA), and journal archives. This approach enables unified querying and knowledge extraction across the entire fragmented biomedical literature ecosystem without requiring a monolithic central warehouse, thereby preserving source autonomy and allowing for the rapid, low-friction integration of new and emerging repositories.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Waveform Analysis to Model Viral Symptom Trajectories for Diagnostic Classification
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from geophysics, where the classification of seismic events relies on analyzing the full temporal evolution of waveforms. The analogous problem of distinguishing viral illnesses based on symptom lists is solved by moving from static feature sets to dynamic trajectory models. The adapted research idea is to develop a diagnostic classification model that treats a patient's reported symptom profile as a multivariate temporal sequence, akin to a seismic waveform. By training machine learning models (e.g., recurrent neural networks or temporal convolutional networks) on longitudinal symptom data—capturing onset order, intensity progression, co-occurrence, and duration—the model can learn pathogen-specific 'signature' trajectories to improve the differentiation of COVID-19 from influenza, RSV, and other common viral infections.

ideas.title: Dynamic Ecological Niche Modeling of Viral Symptomatology from Heterogeneous Real-Time Literature Streams
ideas.description: This research direction integrates ecological niche modeling (ENM) with advanced, multi-source literature parsing to dynamically model the 'symptom space' of viral pathogens. By parsing and structuring symptom data not only from PubMed but also from preprint servers (e.g., medRxiv), clinical trial registries, and case report databases, it creates a rich, temporally granular input stream for ENM algorithms. This allows for the construction of dynamic, high-dimensional niche models that can track how the symptomatic presentation of a virus evolves over time and across different populations, much like tracking a species' range shift. The synergy enables predictive identification of anomalous symptom clusters or emerging clinical phenotypes from early, fragmented reports, offering a novel tool for epidemic intelligence and hypothesis generation.

ideas.title: Dynamic Knowledge Integration with Forgetting-Guarded Evaluation for Continuous Model Currency
ideas.description: This system moves beyond simple periodic retraining to a continuous integration architecture that safeguards against catastrophic forgetting and concept drift. It employs parallel model branches: a stable 'core' and fluid 'experimental' branches trained on new literature. An automated evaluation framework constantly assesses all branches against a multi-faceted benchmark covering foundational biomedical knowledge, recent high-impact findings, and downstream task performance. Updates are merged into the core model only after passing thresholds that prove they enhance currency without eroding established, validated knowledge, ensuring the model remains both current and reliable.

ideas.title: Collaborative filtering for host-immunity-specific symptom-disease mapping
ideas.description: This research adapts collaborative filtering techniques from recommendation systems to model symptom-disease associations not as universal, but as contingent on an individual's immune context (e.g., prior infection/vaccination status for specific pathogens). The system treats different immune history cohorts as distinct 'users,' their symptom profiles as 'ratings,' and diseases as 'items,' thereby learning context-specific predictive patterns. This allows it to infer probable diagnoses for novel symptom presentations by leveraging similarity patterns within and across immune-history groups, effectively filling missing links in personalized diagnostic matrices. The outcome is a predictive framework that dynamically adjusts diagnostic clues based on host immunity, enhancing the accuracy of syndromic surveillance and clinical decision support for infectious and immune-mediated diseases.

ideas.title: Clinician-facilitated pre-visit genomic symptom intake and education platform
ideas.description: This redesigned system is a secure platform where patients can input symptoms and family history prior to a clinical genetics consultation. It provides tailored educational resources about genetic testing limitations and the meaning of potential findings as the user proceeds. The output is not a list of potential diagnoses for the patient, but a structured summary and preliminary differential for the ordering clinician, integrating the patient-reported data with curated genomic knowledge bases. This maintains patient engagement and data quality while ensuring information is reviewed and communicated within an appropriate clinical framework.

ideas.title: Leveraging Contextual Embedding and Semantic Frames for Biomedical Concept Normalization
ideas.description: This research seed proposes to improve phenotype and genotype concept normalization by drawing an analogy from machine translation in computational linguistics. The analogous problem is mapping context-dependent words/phrases between languages, which is solved using contextual embeddings and semantic frame analysis (like semantic role labeling) to understand meaning within a sentence structure. Adapted to biomedical informatics, the solution is to develop a novel normalization pipeline that first uses a model like BioBERT to generate context-aware embeddings of clinical note snippets, then applies a biomedical semantic role labeler to extract predicate-argument structures (e.g., identifying the experiencer, symptom, and temporal modifiers). This enriched, structured representation—capturing both contextual nuance and relational semantics—is used to perform a more sophisticated, disambiguated mapping to standardized terms in ontologies like HPO (Human Phenotype Ontology), addressing the limitations of current string-based or simple embedding-based approaches.

ideas.title: Create more diverse few-shot clinical tasks

ideas.sourceTitle: EHRSHOT: An EHR Benchmark for Few-Shot Evaluation of Foundation Models

ideas.title: Use document deduplication for copy-forwarded diagnoses
ideas.description: Adapt NLP methods for detecting and handling near-duplicate text to manage repeated EHR codes.

ideas.title: Anchor symptom reporting bias to objective clinical measures in a joint estimation model
ideas.description: This research develops a model that quantifies differential symptom reporting bias by integrating self-reported screening data with concurrent, objective clinical or biomarker data (e.g., actigraphy, lab tests, clinician-rated severity). The system uses a multi-task Bayesian framework to jointly estimate the latent disease state, the bias function for self-reports across groups, and the performance of the screening instrument. This allows the bias correction to be constrained and validated against objective evidence, producing robust, falsifiable estimates of how reporting differences affect screening accuracy across demographic or clinical subgroups.

ideas.title: Inverse: Use LLM to critique clinician documentation

ideas.title: Multi-Center Pragmatic Trial of AMELIE for Generalizable Workflow Integration and Diagnostic Performance
ideas.description: This redesigned study conducts a prospective, multi-center, pragmatic trial across a diverse network of hospitals and clinics with varying patient demographics, clinical specialties, and IT systems. The system is integrated into the live clinical workflow at each site without imposing artificial research conditions, allowing for the measurement of real-world diagnostic accuracy, time savings, and user adoption. A stepped-wedge cluster design ensures each site acts as its own control, enabling robust analysis of impact while accounting for institutional heterogeneity. The primary outcome is the generalizability of performance improvements, with secondary analyses on the specific technical and human factors that predict success or failure in different environments.

ideas.title: Apply Legal Document Relation Extraction to Biomedical NLP
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from Legal Informatics, where solutions like LegalBERT combined with Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are used to extract and contextualize complex relationships within dense, structured texts. The analogous problem of extracting party-obligation or case-citation networks from legal documents is solved by using domain-specific pre-trained models followed by GNNs to leverage the graph structure of the domain knowledge. Adapted to the biomedical context, the research idea proposes to enhance gene-phenotype relationship extraction by not only using a model like BioBERT to identify candidate relations from text but also by integrating a GNN layer. This GNN would be trained on existing biological knowledge graphs, allowing it to use the network context to refine, validate, and connect the extracted relationships, leading to a more accurate and semantically rich representation of the gene-phenotype landscape from the literature.

ideas.title: Use causal inference methods for treatment effect

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Modeling to Map Symptom Profiles by Immune History
ideas.description: This idea adapts a core method from ecology—stratified species distribution modeling—to challenge the assumption that a single symptom profile best predicts COVID-19 across all individuals. In ecology, the environmental niche for a species can vary between populations due to adaptation; analogously, the 'symptom niche' for SARS-CoV-2 may vary between host populations with different immune histories (infection-naive, vaccinated, previously infected). The analogous solution is to build separate, stratified predictive models for each group. The adapted research would involve collecting detailed symptom data alongside confirmed immune histories from a large cohort, then training and comparing the performance of stratum-specific machine learning diagnostic models against a generic one. This tests if immune history changes the predictive 'landscape' of symptoms, potentially leading to more personalized and accurate clinical screening tools.

ideas.title: Credibility-Aware Parsing and Integration of Multi-Source Biomedical Literature
ideas.description: This system integrates diverse sources like preprint servers and clinical trial registries but does so through a foundational credibility-assessment layer. Before deep parsing, each document is evaluated on dimensions including source type, methodological rigor indicators, and linkage to established knowledge. The core parsing and information extraction engines are then confidence-weighted, ensuring robust findings are prioritized. The final output is a synthesized knowledge graph where every claim is annotated with its source provenance and a computed confidence score, enabling nuanced, evidence-aware literature analysis.

ideas.title: Apply domain adaptation techniques from computer vision

ideas.title: Gene-Disease Link Discovery via Unsupervised Pattern Recognition and Causal Inference
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that novel gene-disease links must be anchored in published literature. Drawing an analogy from astrophysics, where exoplanets were discovered through indirect pattern recognition in large datasets (e.g., stellar transit dimming), we propose a system that treats undiscovered gene-disease relationships as hidden signals. The adapted solution involves creating an unsupervised computational pipeline that analyzes multi-modal patterns—such as the statistical co-occurrence of rare genetic variants with specific phenotypic trajectories in electronic health records (EHRs) or biobank data—without relying on prior published associations. Candidate links are flagged based on robust, reproducible patterns and causal inference scoring, then prioritized for experimental validation, effectively bypassing the literature-first bottleneck to accelerate novel discovery.

ideas.title: Veterinary Genetic Diagnosis via Multi-Source Evidence Integration and Active Curation
ideas.description: This system overcomes data fragmentation in veterinary genetics by building an integrated knowledge base from structured and unstructured sources, including published literature, electronic health records, diagnostic lab reports, and breed registry data. It employs specialized NLP to extract phenotypic and genotypic associations from clinical text and combines them with actively curated data partnerships. A probabilistic evidence-weighting model assesses the reliability of each source to populate a unified veterinary genetics knowledge graph. Diagnostic pipelines then query this robust, multi-evidence graph to deliver accurate genetic disease assessments for companion and livestock animals.

ideas.title: Invert the data scarcity premise

ideas.title: Neuro-Symbolic, Active Learning Framework for Robust Gene-Phenotype Knowledge Discovery
ideas.description: This system combines a deep neural relation extractor (e.g., BioBERT) with a symbolic reasoning engine grounded in biomedical ontologies like GO and HPO to infer implicit and validate explicit gene-phenotype relationships. An active learning pipeline identifies low-confidence, novel, or contradictory extractions, presenting them to domain experts for rapid curation. This human feedback is used to iteratively retrain the neural model and refine the symbolic rules, creating a closed-loop system that improves both precision and recall over time and adapts to new linguistic patterns and biological knowledge.

ideas.title: Prospective Validation of AMELIE Using a Phased Deployment Framework from Aerospace
ideas.description: This idea adapts the phased certification framework used in aerospace for new aircraft systems to the prospective clinical validation of the AMELIE diagnostic system. The analogous aerospace solution involves sequential testing in simulators, on test aircraft, in shadow mode on commercial flights, and finally in controlled real-world operations. Adapted to clinical research, this translates to a multi-phase prospective study. First, AMELIE would operate in a 'silent/shadow mode' within the live clinical setting, collecting performance data without influencing care. Subsequently, a controlled, instrumented rollout would be conducted in a specific clinical module, where AMELIE's diagnostic suggestions are actively integrated into the workflow. This phased approach systematically measures diagnostic accuracy, time savings, and human-AI interaction dynamics in a real-world environment, mitigating risk and building a robust evidence base for clinical utility before full-scale deployment.

ideas.title: A Veterinary Variant Database via Cross-Species Literature Mining
ideas.description: This idea applies an analogical solution from pharmacovigilance to scale veterinary Mendelian genetics. In drug safety, NLP pipelines mine unstructured reports to populate structured databases (e.g., FAERS) for signal detection. We adapt this by building a system to extract genetic variants (genes, mutations) and associated clinical phenotypes from the fragmented corpus of veterinary literature. The output is a centralized, searchable veterinary variant database. This resource will enable the application of association mining techniques to identify novel disease-gene links across companion and livestock animals, directly scaling the diagnostic pipeline by systematizing knowledge currently locked in narrative text.

ideas.title: Dynamic Symptom Threshold Optimization via Adaptive Signal Detection Theory
ideas.description: This system applies receiver operating characteristic (ROC) analysis to optimize symptom-based screening thresholds but integrates it with a continuous learning infrastructure. It continuously ingests data from syndromic surveillance and confirmed diagnostic outcomes to monitor the real-world predictive value of symptoms. The system uses statistical process control charts to detect significant drift in sensitivity or specificity, automatically triggering the recalibration of symptom weights and decision thresholds. This creates an adaptive screening tool that maintains optimal performance as disease prevalence and symptom meaning evolve over time and across different populations.

ideas.title: Investigate optimal pretraining data temporal windows

ideas.title: Adapting Radar Detection Thresholds to Medical Screening
ideas.description: This idea adapts a classic solution from radar signal processing—the Neyman-Pearson lemma for optimal detection—to the problem of setting medical screening thresholds. In radar, operators must decide if a blip is a real target or noise, balancing the critical trade-off between missed detections and false alarms under resource constraints. The analogous solution fixes an acceptable false alarm rate and then finds the detection threshold that maximizes the hit rate. Adapted to clinical screening, this method would involve first defining a healthcare system's tolerable false positive rate (e.g., based on the capacity for confirmatory testing or specialist referral), then using Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) analysis to identify the precise symptom score or biomarker cutoff that maximizes the detection of true cases (sensitivity) without exceeding that predefined false positive burden. This provides a principled, resource-aware framework for optimizing diagnostic decision criteria.

ideas.title: A Multi-Modal Evidence Engine for De Novo Gene-Disease Prioritization
ideas.description: This research direction develops a computational engine that integrates genomic and phenotypic patterns to generate novel gene-disease hypotheses, then directly scores and ranks these candidates using a unified, multi-modal evidence layer. This layer quantitatively combines features from protein structures (e.g., impact of variants on stability or interaction interfaces), biological pathway models (e.g., perturbation of key network nodes), and population genomics data (e.g., constraint scores, allele frequency patterns). The system's core innovation is its closed-loop, evidence-first approach, which generates and validates hypotheses within the same framework, enabling the discovery of novel associations for which no published textual corroboration yet exists.

ideas.title: Challenge that clinician-written responses are the gold standard

ideas.title: Multi-Modal Evidence Fusion for Biomedical Hypothesis Ranking
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that all relevant evidence for ranking biomedical hypotheses exists in textual form. It draws an analogy from Geospatial Intelligence (GEOINT), where analysts solve the 'all-source' problem by fusing evidence from disparate, non-commensurate data streams (e.g., imagery, signals, human reports) using ontological alignment and probabilistic fusion models. The adapted solution is to create a multi-modal evidence fusion system for biomedical research. An ontology would semantically link entities (genes, diseases, variants) across text, 3D protein structures, pathway diagrams, and population genomics tables. A computational framework, inspired by GEOINT fusion engines, would then ingest scores or features from each modality—such as textual relevance, protein-protein interaction interfaces from structures, or allele frequency patterns—and combine them using a formal evidence theory (e.g., Bayesian updating) to produce a unified, robust ranking score that reflects the totality of available evidence, not just the textual subset.

ideas.title: Apply meta-learning for fast adaptation to shift

ideas.title: A Bayesian Network Model for Hybrid Diagnostic Screening Integrating Symptom Reports and Rapid Test Results
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from structural engineering, where hybrid condition assessments combine qualitative visual inspections with quantitative non-destructive test results using probabilistic models. The analogous solution involves using frameworks like Bayesian networks to integrate disparate data types for a more robust overall risk assessment. Adapted to diagnostic screening, the study proposes developing and validating a Bayesian network model that integrates patient-reported symptom profiles (including type, severity, and duration) with quantitative or semi-quantitative rapid antigen test outcomes (e.g., faint line intensity, digital readout if available) to generate a posterior probability of disease. This hybrid strategy aims to improve screening accuracy, inform triage decisions, and reduce missed cases or unnecessary confirmatory testing compared to using either symptoms or the point-of-care test result in isolation.

ideas.title: Dynamic SDT Triage: A Signal Detection Framework for Real-Time, Adaptive Thresholds in Neonatal ICU Monitoring
ideas.description: This research proposes developing a fast, lightweight clinical decision support tool for neonatal ICUs that embeds core principles of Signal Detection Theory (SDT) at its operational core. Instead of using fixed symptom thresholds, the system continuously analyzes incoming streams of physiological and observational data to calculate context-sensitive decision criteria, dynamically balancing the costs of misses and false alarms. This allows for rapid triage that automatically adapts its alerting behavior based on the current prevalence of critical conditions in the unit and available clinical resources, optimizing diagnostic accuracy under time pressure. The result is a novel, theoretically grounded framework for adaptive triage that maximizes the utility of limited information in high-stakes, dynamic environments.

ideas.title: Rapid Triage via Clinical 'Fast Path' Processing
ideas.description: This research adapts the 'fast path' optimization from computer architecture to create a rapid triage system for neonatal ICU cases. In microprocessors, a simplified circuit handles common instructions quickly, while complex ones take a slower route. Analogously, we propose developing a clinical 'fast path' using a deliberately limited, ultra-rapid diagnostic panel (e.g., 3-4 key biomarkers from a point-of-care device) designed solely to detect the most prevalent and time-critical neonatal conditions. This system would provide a binary 'critical/not-critical' triage decision within 5-10 minutes, enabling immediate intervention for the most urgent cases. The standard, more comprehensive (but slower) diagnostic workup would run concurrently as the 'slow path' for detailed diagnosis and management of all patients, ensuring no loss of information while dramatically accelerating the initial life-saving decision point.

ideas.title: Investigate optimal continued pretraining data volume and strategies
