ideas.title: Apply Market Segmentation Analysis to Viral Symptom Space
ideas.description: This idea adapts market segmentation techniques from consumer analytics to virology and clinical medicine. In marketing, algorithms analyze multi-dimensional consumer data to identify distinct, targetable groups. Analogously, we can apply clustering and latent class modeling to a dataset of viral infections, where each case is defined by a vector of symptoms (the 'preference' data) and patient characteristics (the 'demographic' data). The solution would identify and characterize natural clusters or 'segments' within the symptom space—such as a 'fever-dominant respiratory' segment or a 'neuro-gastrointestinal' segment—that may cross traditional viral family boundaries. This provides a data-driven, phenotypic framework for comparing outbreaks, generating hypotheses about shared pathogenic mechanisms, and developing segment-specific diagnostic or management protocols.

ideas.title: A Predictive, Plausibility-Filtered System for Prioritizing High-Value Research Gaps
ideas.description: This system moves beyond simple identification of under-studied entities to actively prioritize the most promising research opportunities. It first aggregates evidence from diverse sources, including functional genomic data, evolutionary conservation, and molecular interaction networks, to estimate the biological plausibility of a connection. A machine learning model, trained on historical publication and experimental outcome data, then scores and ranks the identified gaps based on their predicted tractability and potential impact. The final output is a shortlist of high-confidence, mechanistically plausible research targets, effectively filtering out noise and focusing resources on the most viable opportunities.

ideas.title: A Dynamic, Value-Based Platform for Patient-Centric Genetic Symptom Exploration and Resource Allocation
ideas.description: This research develops an integrated platform that combines a patient-facing symptom exploration tool with a backend economic simulation model. As patients input symptoms to explore potential genetic causes, the platform dynamically incorporates anonymized, aggregated data into a cost-effectiveness model that evaluates testing and care pathways. This allows the tool to provide users with not only potential genetic insights but also context about the public health value and resource implications of different diagnostic journeys. The synergy enables a novel form of participatory health economics, where patient engagement feeds continuous policy optimization, and transparent economic context empowers more informed patient choices.

ideas.title: Causal dynamic modeling of viral symptom profiles with bias adjustment
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond static ecological analogies to model symptom profiles as dynamic, causally structured processes. It employs time-series Bayesian networks to represent how symptom complexes evolve, while incorporating latent variable models to adjust for surveillance biases and healthcare-seeking behavior. The framework explicitly tests causal hypotheses about viral etiology using counterfactual reasoning and propensity score methods on longitudinal health data. This creates a robust system for identifying pathogen-specific symptom signatures that are invariant to contextual noise and diagnostic drift.

ideas.title: Diagnose and adapt symptom screening mechanisms using causal perturbation across contexts
ideas.description: This research employs an active causal discovery framework to move beyond assessing generalizability to diagnosing and fixing its failures. It integrates deliberate experimental perturbations of screening delivery, environment, and phrasing with dense measurement of individual and contextual variables within diverse populations. Using causal inference and mechanism modeling, the system isolates whether performance gaps stem from linguistic interpretation, clinical presentation, access factors, or tool structure. The final output is a set of validated, context-specific adaptation rules for the screening tool, along with a diagnostic protocol to preemptively identify high-risk settings for future deployments.

ideas.title: Adapting Geologic Sensor Fusion to Augment Symptom Screening
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that self-reported symptoms are the primary valid screening tool by proposing a multi-source digital phenotyping framework. The analogy is drawn from geophysics, where the integration of data from disparate sensor arrays (seismometers, magnetometers, GPS) through fusion techniques solves the problem of specificity in identifying subsurface events. Adapted to health screening, the research would develop and validate algorithms to fuse passive data streams from wearables and smartphones—such as keystroke dynamics, ambient audio, mobility, and physiological sensors—creating a composite digital signature. This fused signature aims to significantly improve the specificity of detecting and differentiating clinical symptoms, moving beyond the noise and bias of single-metric approaches or self-report.

ideas.title: Adapting Portfolio Optimization for Symptom-Based Screening Triage
ideas.description: This research adapts the core solution from Modern Portfolio Theory in financial economics to the problem of symptom-based screening prioritization. In finance, MPT mathematically optimizes asset allocation by modeling the trade-off between expected return and risk, identifying an efficient frontier of optimal portfolios. We translate this to public health by conceptualizing different patient symptom profiles (e.g., specific clusters, severity scores, or syndromic categories) as analogous to financial assets. Each profile has an associated expected 'health return' (e.g., detected cases, downstream transmissions prevented) and 'risk' (e.g., variability in outcome, chance of missing a severe case). By constructing a health portfolio optimization model, we can identify the efficient frontier of screening policies—those that maximize aggregate health benefit for any given level of accepted risk or resource constraint. This provides a rigorous, quantitative framework for policymakers to evaluate the cost-effectiveness trade-offs of different symptom-based triage protocols, moving beyond simple thresholds to a systematic optimization of a mixed-strategy screening portfolio.

ideas.title: Integrating Continuous Digital Phenotypes with Polygenic Models to Deconvolute Complex Symptom Etiology
ideas.description: This research direction develops a framework that integrates continuous, multi-modal data from wearable sensors and digital phenotyping (e.g., activity, sleep, heart rate variability) with methods for modeling the combinatorial effects of genetic variants. Instead of relying on coarse self-reported symptoms, the approach uses finely-grained, objective symptom dynamics as the phenotypic target for association. The core innovation is to rank and validate polygenic models not against a static diagnosis, but against specific, time-varying digital phenotype signatures, thereby uncovering how different combinations of genetic variants manifest as distinct real-world physiological and behavioral patterns over time.

ideas.title: Temporal-polygenic phenotyping for syndromic differentiation
ideas.description: This research direction develops computational models that integrate the detailed temporal progression of symptoms (order, duration, severity) with multi-gene variant data to differentiate between syndromes with overlapping presentations, such as COVID-19, influenza, and monogenic mimics. The method treats the symptom timeline as a phenotypic trajectory and uses it to constrain and rank polygenic or oligogenic variant combinations, identifying those that mechanistically explain the specific clinical course. This enables the simultaneous classification of the active syndrome and the discovery of genetic architectures that predispose to or modulate its unique temporal expression, offering a powerful tool for diagnosing complex post-infectious or inflammatory conditions.

ideas.title: Relax reliance on published literature for novel genes

ideas.title: Veterinary Genomic Discovery Engine: De novo gene-disease association in non-model species
ideas.description: This research fuses the application of a human genetics pipeline to veterinary medicine with methods for proposing novel gene-disease links, creating a Veterinary Genomic Discovery Engine. The system is designed to analyze genomic data (e.g., from biobanks, veterinary clinics, or livestock operations) alongside structured phenotypic data (e.g., electronic health records, owner-reported traits) to identify statistically robust genotype-phenotype associations de novo, even for genes with no prior published disease link in that species. This directly addresses the critical bottleneck in veterinary genetics: the sparse and lagging literature for many companion and livestock animals. The outcome is a proactive diagnostic and discovery tool that can propose candidate causal variants for both rare Mendelian disorders and complex traits in animals, accelerating genetic diagnosis and breeding programs without dependence on existing publications.

ideas.title: Calibrating Screening Estimates with Latent Class Models for Differential Symptom Reporting
ideas.description: This idea inverts the assumption of uniform symptom reporting by adapting a solution from ecology. Ecologists solve the analogous problem of estimating species presence when detection is imperfect and variable using occupancy-detection models. We adapt this by treating disease status as a latent variable and symptom reporting as a detection process with bias. The research would develop a statistical model that uses patient covariates (e.g., age, gender, socioeconomic status, cultural background) to parameterize differential reporting probabilities. This model would be applied to screening data to produce bias-corrected estimates of screening test accuracy and disease prevalence, providing a more valid assessment of a screening program's real-world performance across diverse populations.

ideas.title: Knowledge-Guarded Collaborative Filtering for Gene-Phenotype Associations
ideas.description: This approach adapts recommendation system algorithms but crucially integrates them with structured biological knowledge to overcome the pitfalls of pure phenotypic similarity. It employs a dual-network model that jointly learns from phenotypic association patterns and prior biological networks (e.g., protein-protein interactions, shared pathways). An adversarial validation layer actively penalizes predictions that, while statistically plausible from phenotypic data alone, contradict established mechanistic constraints, ensuring generated gene-disease links are both novel and biologically credible.

ideas.title: Variant-Specific Signal Detection: Optimizing Phenotypic Screening for Genomic Variants
ideas.description: This research direction integrates the genotype-to-phenotype prediction of Idea B with the psychophysical decision theory of Idea A. For a novel genetic variant, the method first synthesizes literature to predict its full spectrum of potential clinical presentations and severities. It then applies signal detection theory, not to generic symptoms, but to this predicted phenotypic profile to mathematically derive an optimal, variant-specific screening threshold that balances the costs of missed cases against false alarms. This results in a precision screening protocol where the clinical decision criterion is dynamically tailored to the expected biological signal of the variant in question.

ideas.title: Dual-Path Assurance Network for Neonatal ICU Critical Triage
ideas.description: This system employs a two-component AI architecture for neonatal ICU monitoring. A primary, ultra-low-latency 'Triage Filter' provides immediate, high-confidence alerts for clear critical events. Simultaneously, a secondary 'Deep Scrutineer' model performs a more thorough, continuous analysis on the same multimodal data, focusing on subtle and complex patterns. The Scrutineer's outputs are fused with the Triage Filter's, allowing it to issue urgent overrides if it detects a high-risk condition the faster model missed, creating a robust safety net. This design ensures rapid response is maintained without compromising diagnostic depth in life-threatening scenarios.

ideas.title: Adapt ecological niche modeling to validate symptom screening across diverse populations
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from ecology's 'ecological niche modeling' (ENM), where models of species distribution are validated for transferability to new geographic and climatic regions. The analogous problem is testing a model's generalizability beyond its original data context. The adapted research idea applies this framework to medical symptom screening tools. By treating varied demographic groups and geographic settings as distinct 'ecological niches,' researchers can systematically quantify a screening model's 'transferability.' This involves developing a protocol to test the model's performance (e.g., sensitivity, specificity, predictive values) across these diverse niches, thereby rigorously assessing generalizability and identifying the specific population or setting characteristics where validation or recalibration is needed.

ideas.title: Implement a Continuous Integration/Continuous Delivery (CI/CD) Pipeline for Literature-Driven Model Updates
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from Software Engineering/DevOps, where Continuous Integration/Continuous Delivery (CI/CD) pipelines automate code updates and deployment, this idea adapts that solution to the challenge of maintaining AMELIE's current knowledge. The analogous solution involves automated testing and staged deployment of software updates. The adapted research idea is to implement a CI/CD-style pipeline for literature-driven model updates. This automated system would continuously monitor designated literature sources, preprocess and validate new publications, trigger incremental model training in an isolated testing environment, and automatically deploy the updated model to the production system only after it passes a suite of evaluation metrics comparing its performance against the previous stable version. This approach ensures AMELIE's knowledge remains current through a reliable, automated, and quality-gated process, minimizing manual oversight and reducing the risk of deploying a degraded model.

ideas.title: Optimizing Isolation-Triggering Symptom Screens via Signal Detection Theory
ideas.description: This research synthesizes the concept of using symptom screening to directly trigger isolation protocols with the analytical rigor of signal detection theory (SDT). It moves beyond using screening merely to prioritize diagnostic testing, instead applying receiver operating characteristic (ROC) analysis to optimize the symptom-based decision criterion for the isolation action itself. The novel direction involves modeling the costs and benefits of isolation decisions—factoring in transmission dynamics, resource availability, and societal impact—to calculate an optimal threshold that maximizes net utility for a given outbreak context. This provides a principled, adaptive method for public health agencies to set and justify screening rules that balance infection control against the burdens of isolation.

ideas.title: Context-Aware Network Propagation for Polygenic Disease Gene Prioritization
ideas.description: This system moves beyond simple literature ranking to prioritize genes in complex diseases by integrating mined literature with functional biological networks. It first uses literature mining to identify disease-associated biological contexts and an initial gene set. A network propagation algorithm then scores genes based on their connectivity within these disease-relevant contexts, using quantitative functional genomics data as edge weights. This approach suppresses noisy, isolated associations highlighted in literature while promoting genes that are centrally connected to cohesive disease modules, even if they are less frequently published.

ideas.title: Cross-Species Multi-Modal Phenotype-Genotype Fusion for Veterinary Genetics
ideas.description: This research develops a multi-modal fusion framework, inspired by computer vision techniques, specifically tailored for veterinary Mendelian genetics. It integrates heterogeneous data—including unstructured veterinary clinical notes, medical imaging (e.g., radiographs), genomic variant data, and published literature—into a unified, cross-species patient representation. By applying this framework at scale across companion and livestock animals, the system can identify conserved phenotypic expressions of orthologous genetic disorders. This approach not only enhances diagnostic accuracy in veterinary care but also generates a comparative knowledge base that can inform the study of human Mendelian diseases through evolutionary parallels.

ideas.title: Symptom-to-Gene Mapping via Interactive Constraint Satisfaction
ideas.description: This research adapts the constraint-satisfaction and interactive visualization paradigm from computational chemistry's molecular docking tools to create a patient-facing genetic symptom explorer. In molecular docking, users filter millions of molecular conformations by applying interactive constraints (e.g., bond types, spatial limits) to find plausible fits. Similarly, this tool would allow patients to input their symptoms as 'constraints' against a vast genomic-phenotypic knowledge graph. The interface would let users add, remove, or prioritize symptoms, and in response, it would filter, rank, and visually present potential genetic associations in an understandable, dynamic format. This transforms a complex diagnostic query into an interactive, exploratory process, giving patients agency and insight into how their specific symptom profile maps to possible genetic causes, scaling both the usability for patients and the underlying computational filtering logic.

ideas.title: Modeling the Biased Ecological Niche of Pathogens in Symptom-Social Space
ideas.description: This research direction integrates ecological niche modeling (ENM) with models of differential symptom reporting bias. It treats the symptom space as an environment where pathogens occupy a 'fundamental niche' defined by true biological presentation. However, the 'realized niche' observed in surveillance data is distorted by social and demographic gradients in symptom recognition and reporting. By fusing ENM techniques with bias models, the approach can map these distortions, estimate the true underlying symptom profiles, and generate population-specific screening performance estimates that account for local reporting ecology.

Crucially, it then uses a generative model of biological mechanisms—trained on protein interactions, pathways, and cellular processes—to evaluate each candidate for functional plausibility. The output is a prioritized shortlist of hypotheses where the phenotypic association is supported by a plausible, testable mechanistic link to the disease biology. This approach moves beyond pure correlation to produce causally coherent, high-confidence candidates for experimental validation.

ideas.title: A Dual-System for Literature Gap Identification via Citation Network Anomaly Detection
ideas.description: This expanded idea adapts the anomaly detection methodology from astrophysics, specifically exoplanet discovery, to the problem of identifying literature gaps in genetics and phenomics. In exoplanet detection, astronomers infer unseen planets by analyzing systematic perturbations (like stellar wobble or dimming) in well-observed star data. Analogously, this dual-system approach would not search for direct statements about gaps but would instead computationally analyze large-scale bibliometric data to detect 'citation network anomalies.' It would flag phenotypes or genes that exhibit a mismatch between their perceived importance (e.g., high mention frequency in textbooks, databases, or secondary reviews) and their support from a robust, interconnected network of recent primary research citations. These entities, existing in 'citation shadows,' would be systematically highlighted as high-confidence, data-driven opportunities for new research, scaling the identification process beyond manual literature review.

ideas.title: Network Resilience Analysis for Polygenic Disease Gene Prioritization
ideas.description: This idea adapts solutions from ecology and network science, where the problem of identifying critical components in complex systems is analogous to finding key genes in polygenic diseases. Ecologists solve the problem of predicting ecosystem collapse by analyzing network structure to identify keystone species, using metrics of centrality and simulated removal to quantify each node's role in system resilience. We adapt this by modeling the genetic architecture of a non-Mendelian complex disease as an interaction network (e.g., protein-protein, co-expression). The literature mining and ranking framework is scaled up by integrating these network data and applying adapted ecological resilience algorithms. The output prioritizes 'keystone genes'—not necessarily the strongest individual GWAS hits, but those whose predicted dysfunction causes the greatest destabilization of the disease-associated network module, thereby capturing high-impact polygenic contributors in multifactorial disorders.

ideas.title: Disentangle host immunological history from viral co-circulation dynamics to quantify true screening efficacy
ideas.description: This study moves beyond correlating screening efficacy with a dynamic viral mix by explicitly modeling the confounding role of host immunological states. It combines high-resolution community virome surveillance with longitudinal collection of host biospecimens to measure virus-specific and cross-reactive antibody titers, T-cell assays, and host-response biomarkers from the same population. A causal inference model, using these immunological parameters as mediators and effect modifiers, quantifies the direct effect of the shifting viral mix on screening test performance. This approach isolates the true virological signal from the substantial noise introduced by heterogeneous and dynamic host immunity, providing a robust, mechanistic assessment of screening efficacy.

ideas.title: Symptom-Triggered Rapid Multiplex Testing as a Gatekeeper for Isolation Protocols

The system is designed for settings like schools, workplaces, or airports. A positive symptom screen prompts the rapid test, and only a confirmatory positive test result initiates formal isolation protocols and further diagnostic follow-up. This approach minimizes unnecessary isolation from non-infectious symptoms while still providing a faster, more targeted response than waiting for centralized lab testing.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Niche Modeling to Simulate Dynamic Viral Interference in Screening Efficacy
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption of a static co-circulating virus mix when evaluating screening efficacy. By drawing an analogy from Community Ecology, we adapt the solution of dynamic, multi-species population modeling. Ecologists successfully model how fluctuating populations of competing species interact over time using frameworks that incorporate seasonal drivers and competition coefficients. Adapted to virology, this involves creating a simulation where different respiratory viruses are modeled as competing species vying for the susceptible host population. The model integrates seasonal forcing, viral interference mechanisms, and real-world epidemiological parameters to generate a dynamic, time-varying profile of virus prevalence. Screening test performance (e.g., sensitivity, specificity, predictive values) for a pathogen of interest can then be rigorously assessed under these more realistic, fluctuating conditions, revealing how seasonal ebbs and flows of other viruses impact practical screening utility.

ideas.title: Adapting Social Network Friend Recommendation to Gene-Phenotype Association
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from social network friend recommendation to the problem of inferring missing gene-phenotype associations. In social networks, collaborative filtering recommends new friends by analyzing a user's explicit connections and identifying patterns of mutual friends or shared community membership. We translate this by modeling genes as nodes in a bipartite network with phenotypic traits. The 'friendship' of a gene is its set of known phenotypic associations. By applying network-based collaborative filtering algorithms—similar to those used for 'people you may know'—we can infer latent gene-phenotype links. This is done by calculating similarity between genes based on the overlap and structure of their phenotypic profiles and then recommending new phenotype 'connections' for a gene based on the strong associations of its most phenotypically similar neighbor genes, effectively performing a biological 'friend-of-a-friend' recommendation.
